name: 2 Willow Road
description: 2 Willow Road is a groundbreaking Modernist home designed and built by architect Erno Goldfinger in 1939 for his own family. The house showcases his innovative approach to space, light, and materials, and contains the Goldfingers' impressive collection of modern art by Henry Moore, Max Ernst, and Bridget Riley. It is the National Trust's first Modernist property.
shortDescription: Erno Goldfinger's pioneering 1939 Modernist home in Hampstead, the National Trust's first of its kind.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: 575 Wandsworth Road
description: 575 Wandsworth Road is a modest terraced house in Lambeth transformed over 25 years by Kenyan-born poet and teacher Khadambi Asalache into an extraordinary work of art. Every surface of the interior is covered with intricate fretwork panels hand-carved from reclaimed timber, creating an immersive decorative environment. Visits are by timed ticket only, limited to small groups to preserve this fragile and unique interior.
shortDescription: Terraced house in Lambeth transformed with extraordinary hand-carved fretwork covering every surface.

name: A La Ronde
description: A La Ronde is a unique 16-sided house built in 1796 for two spinster cousins, Jane and Mary Parminter, inspired by the Byzantine basilica of San Vitale in Ravenna. The interior is filled with extraordinary decorative work including a shell gallery made from 25,000 shells, feather friezes and sand-and-seaweed pictures. The house offers panoramic views across the Exe estuary from its quirky diamond-shaped and hexagonal windows.
shortDescription: Unique 16-sided Georgian house near Exmouth with a shell gallery and panoramic estuary views.

name: Aberdulais Falls
description: Aberdulais Falls is a picturesque waterfall on the River Dulais that has powered industry for over 400 years, from copper smelting to tin plating. The site now houses a hydroelectric scheme generating green energy and a fish pass for migrating sea trout. Visitors can explore the historic waterwheel, turbine, and waterfall viewpoints.
shortDescription: A historic waterfall powering industry for four centuries, now home to a hydroelectric scheme and fish pass.

name: Acorn Bank
description: Acorn Bank is home to the largest collection of medicinal and culinary herbs in the north of England, set within the sheltered walled garden of a former manor house. The surrounding orchards contain over 100 varieties of traditional apple and other fruit trees. Woodland walks lead down to the dramatic Crowdundle Beck waterfall and the remains of a former corn mill.
shortDescription: A walled herb garden with the north's largest medicinal plant collection, near a hidden waterfall.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Aira Force and Gowbarrow Park
description: Aira Force is a spectacular 20-metre waterfall set in ancient woodland on the shores of Ullswater. The surrounding Gowbarrow Park is where Wordsworth and his sister Dorothy famously saw the daffodils that inspired one of the best-known poems in the English language. Stone-arched footbridges span the gorge above the waterfall, offering dramatic viewpoints.
shortDescription: A stunning 20-metre waterfall in ancient woodland, where Wordsworth found his famous daffodils.

name: Alderley Edge and Cheshire Countryside
description: Alderley Edge is a dramatic sandstone escarpment rising above the Cheshire Plain, steeped in legend and mining history dating back to the Bronze Age. Woodland walks thread through beech, pine and oak, passing old copper mines and the Wizard's Well. From the Edge, views stretch across the plain to Manchester and the Peak District hills.
shortDescription: Legendary sandstone escarpment above the Cheshire Plain with ancient mines and woodland walks.

name: Alfriston Clergy House
description: Alfriston Clergy House is a 14th-century thatched Wealden hall house that in 1896 became the very first building acquired by the National Trust, purchased for just £10. This beautifully preserved medieval timber-framed house features a rare chalk and sour milk floor in the hall, and a charming cottage garden filled with traditional plants. Set beside St Andrew's Church on the village green known as the Tye, it provides a delightful picture of rural Sussex life.
shortDescription: The first building acquired by the National Trust in 1896 — a charming 14th-century thatched hall house.

name: Allan Bank and Grasmere
description: Allan Bank is an informal country house above Grasmere that was once home to both William Wordsworth and John Ruskin. Partly damaged by fire in 2011, the house has been left deliberately unrestored to create a relaxed, welcoming space with stunning views over Grasmere lake and vale. Visitors are encouraged to explore freely, play games and enjoy the grounds.
shortDescription: An informal Lakeland house above Grasmere, once home to Wordsworth and Ruskin.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Allen Banks and Staward Gorge
description: Allen Banks and Staward Gorge is the largest area of ancient semi-natural woodland in Northumberland, set in a dramatic gorge carved by the River Allen. Victorian paths wind through the steep wooded valley past a medieval pele tower ruin at Staward. The site is rich in wildlife, including red squirrels, dippers and otters, and is carpeted with bluebells and wild garlic in spring.
shortDescription: Northumberland's largest ancient woodland in a dramatic river gorge with Victorian paths and red squirrels.

name: Anglesey Abbey
description: Anglesey Abbey is a Jacobean-style house built on the site of a 12th-century Augustinian priory, transformed by the 1st Lord Fairhaven into a treasure house. The house contains an outstanding collection of paintings, furniture, and clocks. The 114-acre garden is famous for its winter garden, one of the finest in England, along with its dahlia garden and sweeping avenues.
shortDescription: A priory-turned-treasure house with outstanding collections and one of England's finest winter gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Antony House
description: Antony is an elegant early 18th-century mansion regarded as one of the finest Queen Anne houses in the West Country. Built of silvery grey Pentewan stone, the house contains fine portraits, furniture and textiles spanning 600 years of the Carew Pole family's history. The grounds include formal gardens with topiary, a knot garden and a national collection of daylilies, while the woodland garden leads down to the Tamar estuary with views into Devon.
shortDescription: Elegant early 18th-century Queen Anne mansion with formal gardens and woodland on the Tamar estuary.

name: Ardress House
description: Ardress House is a pretty 17th-century farmhouse in the apple orchard country of County Armagh, enlarged in Georgian times and featuring fine plasterwork by the celebrated stuccodore Michael Stapleton. The house contains a good collection of paintings and period furniture. The farmyard has been restored with traditional breeds of livestock and a display of old agricultural implements.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century Armagh farmhouse with fine Georgian plasterwork and a restored farmyard.

name: Arlington Court
description: Arlington Court is a Regency house set in a beautiful estate in North Devon, home to the Chichester family for over 500 years. The house contains an eclectic collection including model ships, shells and costume. The estate features a Victorian garden, lake, and carriage collection in the stables, as well as ancient woodland and a heronry.
shortDescription: Regency house in North Devon with eclectic collections, a carriage museum and Victorian gardens.

name: Arnside and Silverdale
description: Arnside and Silverdale is an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty on the shores of Morecambe Bay, where limestone pavements, ancient woodland and salt marshes meet the coast. The area is renowned for its rich wildlife including butterflies, wildflowers and wading birds. The Trust cares for Eaves Wood and Jack Scout, both offering walks with spectacular views across the Bay.
shortDescription: A limestone coast on Morecambe Bay with rich wildlife, ancient woods and bay views.

name: Ascott House
description: Ascott is a Rothschild country house near Wing in Buckinghamshire, originally a half-timbered Jacobean farmhouse transformed in the 1870s into a comfortable country retreat. The house contains an exceptional collection of fine paintings, Chinese ceramics and English and French furniture. The gardens are equally remarkable, featuring rare trees, herbaceous borders, a topiary sundial and stunning views across the Vale of Aylesbury.
shortDescription: Rothschild country house near Wing with exceptional gardens, fine art collections and a famous topiary sundial.

name: Ashdown House
description: Ashdown House is a striking 17th-century Dutch-style hunting lodge built by the 1st Baron Craven as a retreat for Elizabeth of Bohemia, the Winter Queen. The tall, compact chalk-block house stands dramatically on the Berkshire Downs, flanked by two pavilions and surrounded by ancient woodland. The rooftop platform offers sweeping views across the downland landscape.
shortDescription: 17th-century Dutch-style hunting lodge on the Berkshire Downs built for the Winter Queen.

name: Ashleworth Tithe Barn
description: Ashleworth Tithe Barn is a magnificent medieval barn built in the 15th century beside the River Severn. With its impressive stone walls and original roof timbers, it is one of the finest surviving tithe barns in England and a Grade I listed building.
shortDescription: A magnificent 15th-century tithe barn beside the River Severn in Gloucestershire.

name: Ashridge Estate
description: Ashridge Estate is a 5,000-acre expanse of woodlands, commons and chalk downland in the Chiltern Hills. The estate features ancient beech woodland, wildflower meadows and the iconic Bridgewater Monument. It is home to a rich variety of wildlife including muntjac deer, badgers and over 60 species of bird. The visitor centre provides information about walks and trails across the estate.
shortDescription: 5,000 acres of Chiltern woodland, chalk downland and commons with a visitor centre and Bridgewater Monument.

name: Attingham Park
description: Attingham Park is an elegant late-18th-century mansion designed by George Steuart for the 1st Baron Berwick, set in 200 acres of parkland landscaped by Humphry Repton. The grand Neoclassical interiors include a picture gallery with an early cast-iron and glass roof designed by John Nash. The walled garden has been restored as a productive kitchen garden, and the deer park is home to a herd of fallow deer.
shortDescription: A Neoclassical mansion in Repton-designed parkland with a Nash picture gallery and deer park.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Avebury
description: Avebury is the largest stone circle in Europe, enclosing the village of Avebury within its massive Neolithic bank and ditch. Built around 2850 BC, the monument is part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site that includes the West Kennet Avenue, Silbury Hill and the West Kennet Long Barrow. The Alexander Keiller Museum on site tells the story of the circle's rediscovery.
shortDescription: Europe's largest stone circle, a Neolithic UNESCO World Heritage Site enclosing a Wiltshire village.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Avebury Manor and Garden
description: Avebury Manor is a Grade I listed house that has been remodelled through five centuries of occupation, from its Tudor origins through Queen Anne, Georgian and early 20th-century transformations. Following a BBC television collaboration, the rooms have been furnished to represent different periods, with visitors encouraged to sit on chairs and touch objects. The manor sits within the UNESCO World Heritage Site of the Avebury stone circle, the largest in Europe.
shortDescription: Tudor manor near the Avebury stone circle with hands-on period rooms spanning five centuries.

name: Badbury Rings
description: Badbury Rings is a spectacular Iron Age hill fort with three concentric rings of massive earthwork ramparts and ditches, thought to date from around 800 BC. The site has legendary associations with King Arthur's Battle of Mount Badon and sits at the junction of two Roman roads. The surrounding grasslands and nearby tree-lined avenue of beech trees form a rich habitat for butterflies, wildflowers, and birdsong.
shortDescription: A triple-ringed Iron Age hill fort with Arthurian legend links and rich wildflower grasslands.

name: Baddesley Clinton
description: Baddesley Clinton is a romantic medieval moated manor house dating from the 15th century, hidden away in the Forest of Arden. The house is notable for its priest holes, where Catholic priests were concealed during the Reformation, and its atmospheric interiors filled with heraldic glass and period furnishings. The grounds include a tranquil lakeside walk, walled garden, and nature trail.
shortDescription: A romantic medieval moated manor house with priest holes hidden in the Forest of Arden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Baggy Point
description: Baggy Point is a dramatic headland near Croyde in North Devon, jutting out into the Atlantic Ocean. The clifftop walk offers stunning views along the coast, and the rocky shores below are rich in marine life and popular with rock climbers.
shortDescription: A dramatic headland near Croyde with stunning Atlantic views and rich marine life.

name: Ballard Down
description: Ballard Down is a chalk ridge extending north from the dramatic Old Harry Rocks on the Dorset coast near Swanage. The down offers panoramic views across Poole Harbour, Bournemouth Bay and the Isle of Wight, with its chalk grassland supporting a rich variety of wildflowers and butterflies. The ridge walk connects the South West Coast Path to Studland Bay and the Purbeck countryside.
shortDescription: Chalk ridge near Swanage with panoramic coastal views extending from Old Harry Rocks to Studland Bay.

name: Barrington Court
description: Barrington Court is a Tudor manor house near Ilminster, one of the first properties acquired by the National Trust in 1907. The house was rescued from near-dereliction by Colonel Arthur Lyle of the Tate & Lyle sugar dynasty, who restored it in the 1920s and filled the adjacent Strode House with his collection of decorative arts. The garden, laid out by Gertrude Jekyll, is one of her finest surviving designs — a series of interconnected garden rooms including a white garden, rose and iris garden, and a productive kitchen garden that still supplies the estate restaurant.
shortDescription: Tudor manor with Gertrude Jekyll gardens and a restored Strode House near Ilminster in Somerset.

name: Basildon Park
description: Basildon Park is a dramatic Palladian mansion built in the 1770s for Sir Francis Sykes, set in 400 acres of parkland and woodland overlooking the Thames Valley. The house was rescued from dereliction in the 1950s by Lord and Lady Iliffe and features beautifully restored interiors. It has been used as a filming location for Downton Abbey and Pride and Prejudice.
shortDescription: A rescued Palladian mansion overlooking the Thames Valley, used as a Downton Abbey filming location.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Bateman's
description: Bateman's is a beautiful 17th-century Jacobean house that was the home of Rudyard Kipling from 1902 until his death in 1936. The rooms remain much as Kipling left them, including the study where he wrote some of his finest works. The garden features a working watermill and the charming Puck's Hill.
shortDescription: Rudyard Kipling's beloved Jacobean home with his study, garden, and working watermill.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Bath Assembly Rooms
description: The Bath Assembly Rooms are a magnificent suite of 18th-century public rooms designed by John Wood the Younger and opened in 1771 as the social heart of Georgian Bath. The rooms include the grand Ball Room, Tea Room and Octagon, with their original chandeliers and fine neoclassical interiors. Featured in Jane Austen's novels Northanger Abbey and Persuasion, the building was restored after World War II bomb damage.
shortDescription: Georgian social venue designed by John Wood the Younger in 1771, featured in Jane Austen's novels.

name: Beatrix Potter Gallery
description: The Beatrix Potter Gallery is housed in a 17th-century building in Hawkshead that was once the office of Potter's husband, the solicitor William Heelis. The gallery displays a rotating selection of Beatrix Potter's original illustrations and watercolours, alongside personal items and memorabilia. The building itself retains its original interior layout as a working solicitor's office.
shortDescription: Gallery of Beatrix Potter's original illustrations in her husband's former Hawkshead office.

name: Belton House
description: Belton House is a grand country house built in 1685-88 for Sir John Brownlow in the graceful style of the English Baroque. The interiors feature exquisite wood carvings, fine porcelain, and silverware collections spanning three centuries. Set within 1,300 acres of parkland and formal gardens, the estate includes an orangery, Italian and Dutch gardens, and an adventure playground.
shortDescription: An elegant late-17th-century country house set in magnificent formal gardens and vast parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Bembridge Fort
description: Bembridge Fort is a Victorian coastal defence built in the 1860s as part of the Palmerston Forts chain to protect against French invasion. The fort features underground tunnels, gun emplacements and offers panoramic views across the Solent from its rooftop.
shortDescription: A Victorian coastal defence fort with underground tunnels and Solent views.

name: Bembridge Windmill
description: Bembridge Windmill is the only surviving windmill on the Isle of Wight, built around 1700 and last used for grinding grain in 1913. The stone tower mill retains much of its original wooden machinery across four floors, offering a fascinating insight into historic milling technology. From the top, there are fine views across Bembridge and out to sea.
shortDescription: The Isle of Wight's last surviving windmill, dating from around 1700 with original wooden machinery.

name: Beningbrough Hall
description: Beningbrough Hall is an imposing Georgian baroque mansion near York, built in 1716 with stunning wood carvings and a dramatic two-storey great hall. The house displays over 100 portraits from the National Portrait Gallery collection. The surrounding gardens and parkland include a walled garden, an orchard, and a wilderness play area for children.
shortDescription: Georgian baroque mansion near York displaying National Portrait Gallery works in grand interiors.

name: Benthall Hall
description: Benthall Hall is a fine 16th-century stone house near Ironbridge, home to the Benthall family and their descendants for over 500 years. The house features elaborate plasterwork ceilings, carved oak staircases and an intriguing collection of geology specimens reflecting the area's rich industrial heritage. The garden is a delight in spring with masses of crocuses, and the surrounding estate includes woodland walks with views over the Severn Gorge.
shortDescription: 16th-century stone house near Ironbridge with fine plasterwork, spring crocuses and Severn Gorge views.

name: Berrington Hall
description: Berrington Hall is an elegant Georgian mansion designed by Henry Holland in the 1770s, set within a Capability Brown landscape of sweeping parkland and a serpentine lake. The refined interiors feature delicate plasterwork ceilings, a Regency laundry, and a collection of paintings and furniture. The walled garden is being restored to its productive heritage, and the parkland offers walks with views to the Welsh mountains.
shortDescription: An elegant Georgian mansion by Henry Holland in a Capability Brown parkland setting.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Biddulph Grange Garden
description: Biddulph Grange Garden is a remarkable Victorian garden that takes visitors on a tour of the world through themed areas including an Egyptian court, a Chinese garden and an Italian terrace. Created by James Bateman in the mid-19th century, it is one of the most unusual and creative gardens in England. The garden also features a stunning dahlia walk and geological gallery.
shortDescription: A theatrical Victorian garden with themed areas transporting visitors from China to Egypt.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Birling Gap
description: Birling Gap is a coastal beauty spot at the foot of the Seven Sisters chalk cliffs in East Sussex, offering direct beach access via a flight of steps cut into the cliff face. The dramatic chalk cliffs are among the fastest-eroding coastline in Europe, revealing millions of years of geological history. The site provides one of the finest viewpoints for the iconic undulating line of the Seven Sisters.
shortDescription: Coastal access point beneath the Seven Sisters chalk cliffs with dramatic views across the eroding coastline.

name: Birmingham Back to Backs
description: Birmingham Back to Backs is the last surviving court of back-to-back houses in the city, telling the stories of the people who lived there from the 1840s to the 1970s. Guided tours reveal rooms furnished and decorated to represent different eras, from a Victorian tailor's workshop to a 1970s sweetshop. The houses provide a vivid and moving picture of working-class life in Birmingham.
shortDescription: The last surviving court of Birmingham's back-to-back houses, spanning 130 years of working-class life.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Blaise Hamlet
description: Blaise Hamlet is a picturesque group of nine thatched cottages arranged around a green in Henbury, Bristol, designed by John Nash in 1811 for retired estate workers of Blaise Castle. Each cottage is unique in design, featuring ornamental chimneys, rustic porches and Gothic details that epitomise the Romantic cottage orné style. The hamlet is considered one of Nash's finest small-scale works and remains privately occupied.
shortDescription: Picturesque cluster of nine thatched cottages designed by John Nash in 1811 for retired estate workers.

name: Blakeney Point
description: Blakeney Point is a four-mile shingle spit extending into the North Sea off the Norfolk coast, one of the finest examples of a coastal spit in Britain. The nature reserve is home to breeding colonies of common and grey seals, and in summer hosts one of the largest tern colonies in England. The shifting shingle landscape and salt marshes support a remarkable diversity of coastal plants and migrant birds.
shortDescription: Norfolk shingle spit and nature reserve renowned for seal colonies and large breeding tern populations.

name: Blewcoat School
description: The Blewcoat School is a charming Queen Anne building built in 1709 as a charity school for poor children in Westminster. Now serving as the National Trust's London gift shop, the building retains its original architectural character with a distinctive statue of a charity boy above the entrance.
shortDescription: A charming 1709 Queen Anne charity school building in the heart of Westminster.

name: Blickling Estate
description: Blickling Estate centres on a magnificent Jacobean redbrick mansion, one of England's great houses, reputed birthplace of Anne Boleyn. The house contains a spectacular long gallery, an outstanding library of rare books, and fine tapestries. The formal garden features parterre beds, an orangery, and a secret garden, while the wider estate offers miles of woodland and lakeside walks.
shortDescription: A magnificent Jacobean mansion, reputed birthplace of Anne Boleyn, with a spectacular library and formal gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Bodiam Castle
description: Bodiam Castle is one of Britain's most picturesque and evocative medieval castles, built in 1385 as both a defence and a comfortable home. The imposing exterior, complete with wide moat and original portcullis, conceals a ruined but atmospheric interior. The surrounding estate offers peaceful riverside walks.
shortDescription: A picture-perfect 14th-century moated castle with a dramatic exterior and atmospheric ruins inside.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Bodnant Garden
description: Bodnant Garden is one of the finest gardens in the United Kingdom, set in 80 acres above the River Conwy with views to Snowdonia. Created over 150 years by four generations of the Aberconway family, it features sweeping Italianate terraces, a dell garden with rare trees and the famous Laburnum Arch. The garden is spectacular in every season, from spring azaleas to autumn colour.
shortDescription: One of Britain's finest gardens, famed for its Laburnum Arch and Snowdonia views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Borrowdale and Derwent Water
description: Borrowdale is one of the most beautiful valleys in the Lake District, stretching from the shores of Derwent Water southward into dramatic mountain scenery. The Trust owns much of the valley including ancient oak woodland at Johnny Wood and the famous Bowder Stone, a 2,000-tonne boulder balanced on one edge. The area offers walks of all levels from gentle lakeside strolls to high fell routes.
shortDescription: One of the Lake District's most beautiful valleys, with ancient woodland and dramatic mountain scenery.

name: Boscastle
description: Boscastle is a picturesque harbour village on the north Cornwall coast, set in a narrow, winding inlet flanked by towering cliffs. The medieval harbour, rebuilt after devastating floods in 2004, is one of the few safe havens on this exposed coastline. The surrounding clifftop walks offer spectacular views and access to some of Cornwall's wildest coastal scenery.
shortDescription: Historic harbour village on the north Cornwall coast set in a dramatic cliff-flanked inlet.

name: Botallack Mine
description: Botallack Mine is one of Cornwall's most iconic tin and copper mining sites, perched dramatically on the cliffs above the Atlantic Ocean. The Crown Engine Houses, built precariously on the cliff face, are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and symbol of Cornwall's industrial heritage.
shortDescription: Iconic clifftop tin mine ruins and UNESCO World Heritage Site on the Cornish coast.

name: Bourne Mill
description: Bourne Mill is an elaborate Elizabethan fishing lodge built in 1591 from materials salvaged from the dissolved St John's Abbey in Colchester. The striking Dutch-gabled building was later converted to a working watermill, and the overshot waterwheel still turns today. The tranquil millpond and surrounding garden create a peaceful setting on the outskirts of Colchester.
shortDescription: Ornate Elizabethan fishing lodge in Colchester converted to a watermill with a working waterwheel.

name: Box Hill
description: Box Hill is a prominent chalk hill in the Surrey Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, named after the ancient box woodland on its steep slopes. The summit offers spectacular views over the Weald and was famously featured in the 2012 Olympic cycling road race. It is a popular destination for walking, cycling, and wildlife spotting.
shortDescription: A chalk hilltop with sweeping Weald views, ancient box woodland, and popular walking trails.

name: Bradley Manor
description: Bradley Manor is a small medieval manor house tucked away in a wooded valley on the outskirts of Newton Abbot, a surprising survivor amid modern suburbia. Dating mainly from the 15th century, the house features a great hall, a chapel with original wagon roof, and a buttery, all preserving the intimate scale and atmosphere of late medieval domestic life. The house was continuously occupied for over 500 years by the Yarde and Whitley families before being given to the National Trust in 1938. The surrounding meadow garden is managed traditionally for wildflowers and butterflies.
shortDescription: 15th-century manor house hidden in a Newton Abbot valley, with a medieval chapel and wildflower meadow.

name: Brancaster Estate
description: Brancaster Estate encompasses a stretch of the north Norfolk coast including saltmarshes, sand dunes and the tidal Brancaster Harbour. The estate protects important habitats for wading birds, wildfowl and nesting terns along this Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. At low tide, visitors can walk across to Scolt Head Island, a remote shingle and sand barrier island that is one of Britain's finest nature reserves.
shortDescription: North Norfolk coastal estate with saltmarshes, harbour and access to remote Scolt Head Island.

name: Bredon Barn
description: Bredon Barn is a magnificent 14th-century tithe barn near the village of Bredon in Worcestershire. With its impressive stone construction and massive interior, it is one of the finest medieval barns in England and a testament to the agricultural wealth of the region.
shortDescription: A magnificent 14th-century tithe barn near Bredon in Worcestershire.

name: Brimham Rocks
description: Brimham Rocks is a collection of extraordinary weathered gritstone formations scattered across Nidderdale moorland above the Nidd Valley. Sculpted over 320 million years by ice, wind and water, the rocks form dramatic towers, arches and balanced boulders with names like the Idol, the Sphinx and the Dancing Bear. The site offers family-friendly scrambling and sweeping views of the Yorkshire Dales.
shortDescription: Extraordinary weathered gritstone formations on Nidderdale moorland with panoramic Yorkshire views.

name: Brockhampton Estate
description: Brockhampton Estate centres on a picturesque medieval moated manor house with a rare detached half-timbered gatehouse dating from the late 15th century. The surrounding 1,700-acre estate includes traditional orchards, ancient woodland, and farmland in a quiet Herefordshire valley. Seasonal trails wind through bluebell woods and along streams, making it a haven for wildlife and walkers alike.
shortDescription: A medieval moated manor house with a rare timber gatehouse set in a tranquil Herefordshire estate.

name: Brownsea Island
description: Brownsea Island is a 500-acre island in the middle of Poole Harbour, famous as the birthplace of the Scout and Guide movements, where Robert Baden-Powell held his first experimental camp in 1907. The island is a haven for rare red squirrels and a diverse range of wading birds in its saltwater lagoon. Visitors reach the island by boat and can explore woodland trails, beaches, and the Dorset Wildlife Trust nature reserve.
shortDescription: An island nature reserve in Poole Harbour, birthplace of the Scouting movement and home to red squirrels.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Buckland Abbey
description: Buckland Abbey is a 700-year-old former Cistercian monastery that became the home of Sir Francis Drake in 1581. The abbey church was ingeniously converted into a house, and the property displays Drake memorabilia including his famous drum. Set in the Tavy Valley, the estate includes a medieval tithe barn, herb garden, and craft workshops.
shortDescription: Sir Francis Drake's monastery home in the Tavy Valley with his famous drum on display.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Buscot Park
description: Buscot Park is a late 18th-century neoclassical house set in sweeping parkland on the Oxfordshire-Gloucestershire border. The house contains the Faringdon Collection of paintings, furniture and ceramics, including Burne-Jones's famous Briar Rose series. The Italianate water garden, designed by Harold Peto, connects the house to the lake through a series of descending pools and canal.
shortDescription: Neoclassical house with the Faringdon Collection and Harold Peto's celebrated Italianate water garden.

name: Buttermere Valley
description: Buttermere Valley contains two of the Lake District's most photogenic lakes, Buttermere and Crummock Water, set beneath dramatic fell scenery. The easy lakeside walk around Buttermere is one of the most popular in the region, passing through a rock tunnel and offering views of Haystacks and Fleetwith Pike. The valley is also the starting point for ascents of several classic Lakeland peaks.
shortDescription: Twin lakes beneath dramatic fells, with one of the Lake District's finest easy lakeside walks.

name: Calke Abbey
description: Calke Abbey is a baroque mansion known as 'the house that time forgot,' filled with collections amassed by the reclusive Harpur-Crewe family over centuries. The unstored rooms reveal peeling wallpaper, cluttered collections, and faded grandeur deliberately preserved by the National Trust. The surrounding 600-acre parkland is a National Nature Reserve with ancient trees and rare wildlife.
shortDescription: A baroque mansion frozen in time, filled with eccentric collections and surrounded by a nature reserve.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Canons Ashby
description: Canons Ashby is an Elizabethan manor house built using the remains of an Augustinian priory, home to the Dryden family for over 450 years. The intimate interiors feature Jacobean plasterwork, Elizabethan wall paintings, and a fine collection of period furniture. The formal gardens include an ancient terraced layout with topiary yews and a medieval priory church.
shortDescription: An intimate Elizabethan manor house built from a medieval priory with beautiful terraced gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Carding Mill Valley
description: Carding Mill Valley is a beautiful valley carved into the Long Mynd, a dramatic moorland plateau in the Shropshire Hills. Named after the textile mills that once operated here, the valley offers walks ranging from gentle strolls along the stream to challenging hikes up to the windswept summit with views across to Wales. The stream that runs through the valley is popular with families for paddling and dam-building, while the surrounding moorland supports skylarks, red grouse and dippers.
shortDescription: Scenic Shropshire valley in the Long Mynd with stream walks, moorland hikes and views to Wales.

name: Carlyle's House
description: Carlyle's House is an elegant Queen Anne terraced house in Chelsea where the Scottish historian and essayist Thomas Carlyle and his wife Jane lived from 1834 until their deaths. The house is preserved much as the Carlyles knew it, with their books, furniture and personal possessions offering an intimate picture of Victorian literary life. Famous visitors included Charles Dickens, John Ruskin, Alfred Tennyson and Charles Darwin. The small walled garden provides a quiet refuge from the bustle of Chelsea.
shortDescription: Victorian literary home of Thomas Carlyle in Chelsea, visited by Dickens, Tennyson and Darwin.

name: Carnewas at Bedruthan
description: Carnewas at Bedruthan is a dramatic stretch of north Cornwall coastline famed for its towering sea stacks rising from a wide sandy beach below sheer slate cliffs. Legend holds that the giant Bedruthan used the rock stacks as stepping stones across the bay. The clifftop car park and viewing area provide spectacular vistas, though beach access via steep steps is seasonal due to cliff instability.
shortDescription: Dramatic north Cornwall coastline with towering sea stacks legendarily used as a giant's stepping stones.

name: Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge
description: Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge is a famous rope bridge near Ballintoy that spans a 20-metre chasm between the mainland and a small island. Originally erected by salmon fishermen over 350 years ago, the bridge offers breathtaking views of Rathlin Island and the Scottish coast. The surrounding coastal path features dramatic cliff scenery and seabird colonies.
shortDescription: A thrilling rope bridge spanning a coastal chasm with views to Rathlin Island and Scotland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castle Coole
description: Castle Coole is one of Ireland's finest neoclassical houses, designed by James Wyatt and completed in 1798, set in a landscaped parkland near Enniskillen. The interiors feature outstanding Regency furnishings including original silk curtains and a stunning state bedroom prepared for King George IV. The parkland is home to the oldest breeding colony of greylag geese in the British Isles.
shortDescription: One of Ireland's finest neoclassical houses by James Wyatt with Regency interiors near Enniskillen.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castle Drogo
description: Castle Drogo is the last castle to be built in England, designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens and constructed between 1911 and 1930 high above the Teign Gorge on Dartmoor. The granite castle combines medieval grandeur with 20th-century comforts and has undergone a major conservation project. The garden features a formal parterre, circular croquet lawn and rose garden, with dramatic walks through the gorge below.
shortDescription: The last castle built in England, a Lutyens masterpiece above the Teign Gorge on Dartmoor.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castle Ward
description: Castle Ward is an 18th-century mansion in a stunning location overlooking Strangford Lough, famous for its two contrasting architectural facades: classical on the front and Gothic on the garden side. The 820-acre estate includes farmland, woodland, and lakeshore walks. The property is also well known as a Game of Thrones filming location for Winterfell.
shortDescription: An 18th-century mansion with two contrasting facades overlooking Strangford Lough, a Game of Thrones filming location.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Castlefield Viaduct
description: Castlefield Viaduct is a Victorian railway viaduct in the heart of Manchester, reimagined as an elevated park and green space. Inspired by New York's High Line, the viaduct features planters with trees, grasses and wildflowers creating an urban oasis. It offers a unique elevated perspective over the historic Castlefield Basin and the city's canal network.
shortDescription: Victorian railway viaduct in central Manchester transformed into an elevated urban park.

name: Cerne Giant
description: The Cerne Giant is a 55-metre-tall figure of a naked man wielding a club, cut into the chalk hillside above the village of Cerne Abbas. Recent scientific analysis has dated the figure to the late Saxon period, around the 10th century, though its precise origins and meaning remain debated. The figure is best viewed from a designated viewpoint on the opposite hill, and the surrounding chalk downland is rich in wildflowers and butterflies.
shortDescription: A mysterious 55-metre chalk hill figure of ancient origin overlooking the village of Cerne Abbas.

name: Charlecote Park
description: Charlecote Park is an imposing Elizabethan house set in a deer park landscaped by Capability Brown on the banks of the River Avon. The house has been home to the Lucy family since 1247, and legend claims the young William Shakespeare was caught poaching deer here. The Victorian interiors are richly decorated with family treasures, and the park still has herds of fallow and red deer.
shortDescription: An Elizabethan mansion in a Capability Brown deer park, linked to the young Shakespeare.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Chartwell
description: Chartwell was the beloved family home of Sir Winston Churchill for over 40 years. The rooms remain much as he left them, with personal mementos, maps, and gifts from world leaders. The hillside garden offers sweeping views of the Weald of Kent and includes Churchill's painting studio.
shortDescription: Winston Churchill's cherished family home with personal memorabilia and sweeping Weald views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
One adult and up to three children
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
One adult and up to three children

name: Chastleton House
description: Chastleton House is a Jacobean country house built between 1607 and 1612 by a wealthy wool merchant, remarkable for remaining in the same family for nearly 400 years without major alteration. The house is filled with a mixture of rare and everyday objects accumulated over centuries, giving an authentic and unrestored atmosphere quite unlike grander stately homes. The garden features one of England's first croquet lawns — the rules of modern croquet were codified here in 1865 — and topiary dating from the 17th century.
shortDescription: Atmospheric unrestored Jacobean house in the Cotswolds where the rules of modern croquet were written.

name: Cheddar Gorge
description: Cheddar Gorge is Britain's largest gorge, a dramatic limestone ravine carved through the Mendip Hills by glacial meltwater during successive ice ages. The National Trust protects the southern cliffs and surrounding grassland, where rare plants including Cheddar pink grow on the limestone crags. The gorge's cliffs rise to nearly 140 metres and the area is a Site of Special Scientific Interest for its geology and ecology.
shortDescription: Britain's largest gorge, a dramatic limestone ravine through the Mendip Hills with rare Cheddar pink flowers.

name: Chedworth Roman Villa
description: Chedworth Roman Villa is one of the largest and best-preserved Romano-British villas in England, discovered by a gamekeeper in 1864. The remains include outstanding mosaic floors, two bath suites, a water shrine, and underfloor heating systems dating from the 2nd to 4th centuries AD. A modern conservation shelter protects the mosaics while allowing visitors to walk among the ancient rooms.
shortDescription: One of Britain's finest Roman villas with outstanding mosaic floors in a Cotswold woodland setting.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cherryburn
description: Cherryburn is the birthplace of Thomas Bewick, the celebrated 18th-century engraver and naturalist whose exquisite wood engravings of British birds and animals revolutionised book illustration. The small stone farmhouse where Bewick was born in 1753 stands beside the River Tyne at Mickley, and has been preserved with period furnishings alongside a museum displaying his original woodblocks and prints. The farmyard includes a printing workshop where visitors can try Bewick's techniques, and the garden has been planted with the wildflowers and hedgerow plants he so loved to draw.
shortDescription: Birthplace of engraver Thomas Bewick on the Tyne, with a printing workshop and museum of his woodblock art.

name: Chiddingstone
description: Chiddingstone is one of the most picturesque village streets in Kent, with a row of beautifully preserved 16th and 17th-century half-timbered houses. The village is named after a large sandstone boulder, the Chiding Stone, said to have been used for public scolding.
shortDescription: One of Kent's most picturesque villages with beautifully preserved Tudor houses.

name: Chirk Castle
description: Chirk Castle is a magnificent medieval fortress completed in 1310, the last of Edward I's chain of castles and the only one that has been continuously inhabited for over 700 years. The interior spans from medieval dungeon to elegant state rooms with fine furniture and tapestries. The award-winning gardens feature clipped yew hedges, herbaceous borders and a stunning wrought-iron gate made by the Davies brothers in 1719.
shortDescription: The last of Edward I's Welsh castles, continuously lived in for over 700 years.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cilgerran Castle
description: Cilgerran Castle is a dramatic Norman fortress perched high above the Teifi Gorge in Pembrokeshire. The castle's twin round towers, dating from the 13th century, have inspired artists including J.M.W. Turner and offer commanding views over the wooded river valley.
shortDescription: A dramatic Norman castle above the Teifi Gorge that inspired Turner's paintings.

name: Cissbury Ring
description: Cissbury Ring is one of the largest hillforts in England, with ramparts enclosing over 60 acres on the South Downs above Worthing. Dating from the Iron Age, the site also contains Neolithic flint mines over 5,000 years old, making it one of the most important archaeological sites in Sussex.
shortDescription: One of England's largest Iron Age hillforts on the South Downs with Neolithic flint mines.

name: Claife Viewing Station and Windermere West Shore
description: Claife Viewing Station is a restored Gothic ruin on the western shore of Windermere, originally built in 1799 for tourists seeking picturesque views. Coloured glass windows once framed the lake in different seasonal moods. The surrounding woodland paths lead through ancient coppiced woods down to the quiet western shore of England's longest lake.
shortDescription: A restored Gothic viewing station on Windermere's tranquil western shore with woodland walks.

name: Claremont Landscape Garden
description: Claremont Landscape Garden is one of the earliest surviving English landscape gardens, created by some of the greatest names in garden design including Vanbrugh, Bridgeman, Kent, and Capability Brown. The garden features a lake, island, turf amphitheatre, and grotto. It was once the garden of Claremont House, a former royal residence.
shortDescription: One of England's earliest landscape gardens, shaped by Vanbrugh, Kent, and Capability Brown.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Claydon House
description: Claydon House is an 18th-century country house renowned for its extraordinary Rococo interiors, including the unique Chinese Room with intricate carvings. The house has a long connection with Florence Nightingale, whose sister married into the Verney family, and a room is dedicated to her memory. The surrounding parkland offers peaceful walks with views across the Vale of Aylesbury.
shortDescription: An 18th-century house with extraordinary Rococo carvings and strong connections to Florence Nightingale.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Clevedon Court
description: Clevedon Court is a remarkably well-preserved medieval manor house with origins dating to the early 14th century, one of the few surviving houses of its period. The house incorporates elements from every century since its construction, including a 13th-century tower, 14th-century great hall and an elegant 18th-century hanging staircase. The terraced garden, perched on a hillside with views across the Bristol Channel to Wales, features rare and tender plants sheltered by ancient yew trees.
shortDescription: One of England's finest medieval manor houses, with 700 years of architecture and terraced gardens overlooking the Bristol Channel.

name: Cliveden
description: Cliveden is a stately home perched above the Thames with formal gardens, woodland, and riverside walks. Once the home of the Astor family and centre of political intrigue, the Italianate mansion is now a luxury hotel while the National Trust manages the spectacular grounds. The parterre, water garden, and Long Garden are among its highlights.
shortDescription: A grand Thames-side estate with Italianate gardens, woodland walks, and a rich political history.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
1 Adult and up to three children

name: Clouds Hill
description: Clouds Hill is the tiny isolated cottage that was the rural retreat of T. E. Lawrence — Lawrence of Arabia — where he sought solitude after his wartime adventures. The spartan rooms remain much as Lawrence left them, filled with his books, gramophone, and personal effects. Lawrence died in a motorcycle accident nearby in 1935, and the cottage offers a uniquely intimate portrait of one of the 20th century's most enigmatic figures.
shortDescription: The tiny rural retreat of T. E. Lawrence, preserved as he left it before his tragic death.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Clumber Park
description: Clumber Park is a vast expanse of parkland, heath and woods covering 3,800 acres in the heart of Sherwood Forest. Once the country estate of the Dukes of Newcastle, the park features a magnificent double lime tree avenue, a serpentine lake, and a striking Gothic Revival chapel. It is one of Nottinghamshire's most popular green spaces for walking and cycling.
shortDescription: A sweeping 3,800-acre parkland in Sherwood Forest with a grand Gothic chapel and serpentine lake.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Coggeshall Grange Barn
description: Coggeshall Grange Barn is one of the oldest surviving timber-framed barns in Europe, dating from around 1140. Originally part of a Cistercian monastery, this remarkable medieval building showcases exceptional craftsmanship and is a scheduled ancient monument.
shortDescription: One of Europe's oldest surviving timber-framed barns, dating from around 1140.

name: Colby Woodland Garden
description: Colby Woodland Garden is a tranquil eight-acre garden set in a secluded valley near Amroth in Pembrokeshire. The garden is known for its spectacular spring displays of rhododendrons, azaleas, and bluebells, along with a walled garden restored to its former glory. A meadow and woodland walk lead down to a stream and peaceful pond.
shortDescription: A tranquil woodland garden in Pembrokeshire known for spectacular spring rhododendron and bluebell displays.

name: Coldrum Long Barrow
description: Coldrum Long Barrow is a Neolithic burial chamber on the North Downs in Kent, dating from around 4000 BC and one of the best-preserved of the Medway Megaliths. The remains of at least 22 individuals were found within the collapsed stone chamber, now surrounded by a ring of fallen sarsen stones. The site commands fine views across the Medway Valley from its position on the scarp edge of the downs.
shortDescription: Neolithic burial chamber on the North Downs dating from 4000 BC, one of the Medway Megaliths.

name: Coleridge Cottage
description: Coleridge Cottage in Nether Stowey is where the poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge lived with his family from 1797 to 1800 and wrote some of his greatest works, including The Rime of the Ancient Mariner and Kubla Khan. The modest cottage has been preserved with period furnishings and contains displays about Coleridge's life and the extraordinary literary circle that gathered in the Quantock Hills, including William and Dorothy Wordsworth who moved to nearby Alfoxden to be close to their friend.
shortDescription: Somerset cottage where Coleridge wrote The Rime of the Ancient Mariner and Kubla Khan in the 1790s.

name: Coleton Fishacre
description: Coleton Fishacre is an Arts and Crafts-style house built in the 1920s for Rupert and Lady Dorothy D'Oyly Carte of Gilbert and Sullivan fame. The house overlooks a luxuriant garden that cascades through a valley to the sea at Pudcombe Cove. Rare and tender plants from around the world thrive in the sheltered microclimate, and the coastal paths offer superb walking.
shortDescription: 1920s Arts and Crafts house with a lush valley garden tumbling to the Devon coast.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Compton Castle
description: Compton Castle is a fortified medieval manor house tucked into a valley near Paignton in Devon. Home to the Gilbert family since 1329, the castle features a Great Hall, chapel, rose garden and reconstructed medieval kitchen. Sir Humphrey Gilbert, half-brother of Sir Walter Raleigh, set sail from here to claim Newfoundland for Queen Elizabeth I in 1583. The castle's imposing curtain wall and towers were added in the 15th and 16th centuries.
shortDescription: Fortified medieval manor house near Paignton, home of the Gilbert family since 1329.

name: Coney Island, Lough Neagh
description: Coney Island is a small wooded island in the southern reaches of Lough Neagh, the largest freshwater lake in the British Isles. The island has evidence of habitation dating back thousands of years including a round tower foundation and early Christian remains. Accessible by boat from Maghery, it offers a unique retreat with bird-rich wetlands and ancient woodland.
shortDescription: A historic wooded island in Lough Neagh with early Christian remains and bird-rich wetlands.

name: Conwy Suspension Bridge
description: Conwy Suspension Bridge is an elegant bridge designed by Thomas Telford and completed in 1826, spanning the River Conwy next to the medieval castle. The bridge features distinctive castellated towers to complement the adjacent castle walls and is a masterpiece of early 19th-century engineering.
shortDescription: Thomas Telford's elegant 1826 suspension bridge beside Conwy Castle.

name: Coombe Hill
description: Coombe Hill is the highest viewpoint in the Chilterns, rising to 260 metres with sweeping views across the Aylesbury Vale to the distant Cotswolds. The summit is crowned by a Boer War memorial and surrounded by chalk grassland rich in wildflowers and butterflies. The hill is part of the Chilterns Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty and offers excellent walking along the Ridgeway National Trail.
shortDescription: Highest point in the Chilterns with a Boer War memorial and panoramic views across the Aylesbury Vale.

name: Corfe Castle
description: Corfe Castle is one of Britain's most iconic ruins, a dramatic Norman fortress perched on a natural chalk hill in the Purbeck Hills. The castle was built by William the Conqueror and later besieged and deliberately demolished by Parliamentarian forces during the English Civil War. The ruins offer commanding views over the surrounding Isle of Purbeck and the village of Corfe Castle below.
shortDescription: An iconic ruined Norman fortress dramatically perched on a chalk hill in the Purbeck Hills.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cotehele
description: Cotehele is a remarkably complete medieval house on the steep wooded banks of the River Tamar. Built between 1485 and 1627 by the Edgcumbe family, its rooms are hung with tapestries and decorated with original armour and furniture. The estate includes terraced gardens, a restored quay with a sailing barge, and the valley garden at Cotehele Mill.
shortDescription: Medieval manor house on the River Tamar with tapestries, terraced gardens and a historic quay.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Coughton Court
description: Coughton Court is a magnificent Tudor house that has been home to the Throckmorton family since 1409. The imposing gatehouse and half-timbered wings house one of the finest collections of Catholic recusant history in England, with direct connections to the Gunpowder Plot. The gardens include a walled garden, riverside walk, and a stunning display garden created by Christina Williams.
shortDescription: A Tudor house with deep Gunpowder Plot connections, home to the same family for over 600 years.

name: Cragside
description: Cragside was the home of Victorian inventor and industrialist Lord Armstrong and was the first house in the world to be lit by hydroelectric power. The dramatic house is perched on a rocky crag above the Debdon Burn, surrounded by one of the largest rock gardens in Europe and over 1,700 acres of forest and moorland. The estate contains over 7 million trees and 40 miles of footpaths.
shortDescription: Lord Armstrong's revolutionary Victorian mansion, the first house lit by hydroelectric power.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Croft Castle
description: Croft Castle has been home to the Croft family for nearly 1,000 years, with the medieval castle walls enclosing elegant Georgian interiors. The estate includes a pre-Norman church, an Iron Age hill fort at Croft Ambrey, and an avenue of 350-year-old sweet chestnuts. The parkland and woodland walks offer far-reaching views across the Welsh Marches.
shortDescription: A castle held by the same family for nearly 1,000 years, with ancient woodland and an Iron Age hill fort.

name: Crom Estate
description: Crom Estate is one of Northern Ireland's most important nature conservation areas, comprising 1,900 acres of woodland, parkland, and wetland on the shores of Upper Lough Erne. The estate is home to rare species including pine martens, red squirrels, and one of Ireland's largest herds of fallow deer. The ruins of the old castle and two ancient yew trees add historical interest.
shortDescription: A 1,900-acre nature conservation estate on Upper Lough Erne with rare wildlife and castle ruins.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cronkhill
description: Cronkhill is a Regency Italianate villa on the Attingham Park estate in Shropshire, designed by John Nash around 1802 and considered the earliest Italianate villa in England. The asymmetric design with its round tower, loggia and overhanging eaves was inspired by the vernacular farmhouses of the Italian countryside. The villa enjoys views across the Severn valley from its elevated position in Attingham's parkland.
shortDescription: Earliest Italianate villa in England, designed by John Nash around 1802 on the Attingham estate.

name: Crook Hall
description: Crook Hall is a medieval manor house set in beautiful gardens in the heart of Durham city. Dating back to the 13th century, it is one of the oldest inhabited houses in the city, with enchanting walled gardens offering views of Durham Cathedral.
shortDescription: A medieval manor house with enchanting gardens and views of Durham Cathedral.

name: Croome
description: Croome is a Georgian mansion whose landscape was the first major commission for the young Lancelot 'Capability' Brown, and whose interiors were designed by Robert Adam. The wartime story of RAF Defford, a secret radar research base on the estate, adds another dimension to the property. The restored parkland features a lake, church, grotto, and panoramic hilltop viewpoint.
shortDescription: Capability Brown's first landscape commission with Robert Adam interiors and a secret wartime past.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Cushendun
description: Cushendun is a picturesque coastal village at the foot of Glendun on the Antrim coast, known for its distinctive Cornish-style cottages designed by Clough Williams-Ellis. The National Trust cares for the village and the surrounding caves, cliffs, and river estuary. The Cushendun Caves, carved from red sandstone, gained fame as a Game of Thrones filming location.
shortDescription: A picturesque Antrim coast village with Cornish-style cottages and red sandstone caves.

name: Dalton Castle
description: Dalton Castle is a 14th-century pele tower in the centre of Dalton-in-Furness, once the administrative seat of the Abbot of Furness Abbey. The three-storey tower was used as a courthouse and prison and now houses a small local history museum. It stands in the town's main square and is open on a limited seasonal basis.
shortDescription: A 14th-century pele tower in Dalton-in-Furness, once the courthouse for Furness Abbey.

name: Danbury Commons & Blakes Wood
description: Danbury Commons and Blakes Wood form an important nature reserve in the Essex countryside, featuring ancient woodland, heathland and wildlife-rich habitats. The area supports rare species including nightingales and is criss-crossed with peaceful walking trails.
shortDescription: Ancient woodland and heathland nature reserve in the Essex countryside.

name: Dapdune Wharf
description: Dapdune Wharf is a restored riverside wharf on the River Wey Navigation in Guildford, where barges were built and repaired from the 17th century until the 1920s. The site tells the story of the Wey Navigation, one of the earliest navigable rivers in England, opened in 1653 to carry goods between Guildford and the Thames. Visitors can explore the barge-building workshop, see the restored Reliance barge, and take boat trips along the navigation through the Surrey countryside. The wharf also serves as the starting point for towpath walks along the Wey.
shortDescription: Restored barge-building wharf on the Wey Navigation in Guildford with boat trips and towpath walks.

name: Derrymore House
description: Derrymore House is a thatched 18th-century cottage orne set in landscaped parkland near Newry in County Armagh. The elegant single-storey house, built around 1776, was where the Act of Union was drafted in 1800. The surrounding demesne features mature trees, a walled garden, and peaceful walks through rolling parkland.
shortDescription: A thatched 18th-century cottage orne near Newry where the Act of Union was drafted in 1800.

name: Derwent Island House
description: Derwent Island House is an 18th-century Italianate villa set on a wooded island in Derwentwater near Keswick. The house was built around 1778 for Joseph Pocklington, an eccentric who staged mock naval battles on the lake. Open to visitors on just five days each year, the island offers a unique perspective of the surrounding Lakeland fells and the lake itself.
shortDescription: Italianate villa on a wooded island in Derwentwater, open to visitors just five days per year.

name: Devil's Dyke
description: Devil's Dyke is a V-shaped valley on the South Downs near Brighton, reputed to be the longest, deepest and widest dry valley in the UK. Legend says the devil dug it to flood the churches of the Weald, and today it offers some of the finest views in Sussex.
shortDescription: A dramatic dry valley on the South Downs near Brighton with legendary origins and fine views.

name: Dinefwr
description: Dinefwr is a historic estate in the Tywi Valley comprising Newton House, a medieval castle, and a stunning deer park designed by Capability Brown. The castle perched on a rocky hilltop was once the seat of the Welsh princes of Deheubarth. The parkland is home to a herd of White Park cattle, one of the rarest breeds in the world.
shortDescription: A Capability Brown deer park estate with Newton House and a medieval Welsh castle.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Divis and Black Mountain
description: Divis and Black Mountain rise above Belfast providing panoramic views across the city, Belfast Lough, and on clear days to Scotland, the Isle of Man, and the Mourne Mountains. The upland heath and bog landscape is an important habitat for birds including meadow pipits, skylarks, and peregrine falcons. Waymarked trails make this a popular walking destination for locals and visitors.
shortDescription: Upland heath above Belfast offering panoramic views across six counties on a clear day.

name: Dolaucothi Gold Mines
description: Dolaucothi Gold Mines is the only known Roman gold mine in Britain, set in the wooded Cothi Valley in Carmarthenshire. Visitors can take guided underground tours through tunnels dating back nearly 2,000 years. The site also offers surface trails and gold panning experiences that reveal the area's rich mining heritage.
shortDescription: The only known Roman gold mine in Britain, offering guided underground tours and gold panning.

name: Downhill Demesne and Mussenden Temple
description: Downhill Demesne is a dramatic clifftop estate on the north coast of County Londonderry, best known for the iconic Mussenden Temple perched on the cliff edge above the Atlantic. Built in 1785 by Frederick Hervey, Earl-Bishop of Derry, as a library, the circular temple was modelled on the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli. The ruins of Downhill House and the walled garden are also within the demesne.
shortDescription: A dramatic clifftop estate with the iconic Mussenden Temple perched above the Atlantic Ocean.

name: Dudmaston Hall
description: Dudmaston Hall is a late 17th-century house set in parkland above a large pool known as the Big Pool near Bridgnorth in Shropshire. The house contains an unusual collection that spans 300 years, from Dutch flower paintings and family portraits to a striking collection of modern art including works by Henry Moore, Barbara Hepworth and Ben Nicholson, collected by Sir George and Lady Rachel Labouchere in the 20th century. The extensive grounds include lakeside walks, a dingle with tree ferns and a productive orchard.
shortDescription: 17th-century Shropshire house with an eclectic mix of Old Masters and modern art beside a tranquil lake.

name: Dunham Massey
description: Dunham Massey is a Georgian house set in a magnificent deer park on the outskirts of Altrincham. The house contains outstanding collections of Huguenot silver, paintings and furniture amassed by the Booth and Grey families. The ancient deer park is home to one of England's largest herds of fallow deer, and the winter garden is celebrated for its bark, berries and scented flowers.
shortDescription: Georgian house in a deer park near Altrincham with Huguenot silver and a famous winter garden.
