Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Dunseverick Castle
description: Dunseverick Castle stands as a dramatic ruin on a rocky headland overlooking the Atlantic on the north Antrim coast between the Giant's Causeway and Ballintoy. Once one of the oldest and most strongly fortified places in Ireland, its origins date back over 1,500 years. Only a single wall of the gatehouse remains, perched above the crashing waves.
shortDescription: Dramatic ruins of an ancient fortification on a rocky headland on the north Antrim coast.

name: Dunstable Downs
description: Dunstable Downs is a chalk escarpment on the northern edge of the Chiltern Hills offering spectacular views across the Vale of Aylesbury. The area is popular for kite flying, walking and wildlife spotting, with its rich chalk grassland supporting numerous butterfly species.
shortDescription: A dramatic chalk escarpment on the Chiltern Hills with panoramic views and rich wildlife.

name: Dunstanburgh Castle
description: Dunstanburgh Castle is a dramatic 14th-century ruin standing on a remote basalt headland on the Northumberland coast, accessible only by foot. Built by Thomas, Earl of Lancaster, in 1313 as both a fortress and a political statement against Edward II, the castle's massive gatehouse and curtain walls dominate the wild coastline. The castle is managed by English Heritage but the surrounding coastline and farmland are in National Trust care.
shortDescription: Dramatic 14th-century castle ruin on a remote Northumberland headland, accessible only on foot.

name: Dunster Castle
description: Dunster Castle is a dramatically sited medieval fortress perched on a steep hill overlooking the Bristol Channel, remodelled in the 19th century as a comfortable country home. The castle has been occupied for over 1,000 years, with a gatehouse, towers, and interiors spanning the medieval, Jacobean, and Victorian periods. The subtropical terraced gardens feature rare plants, a working watermill, and views to Exmoor.
shortDescription: A medieval hilltop castle with subtropical gardens overlooking the Bristol Channel and Exmoor.

name: Dunwich Heath
description: Dunwich Heath is a rare and precious fragment of the lowland heath that once covered much of coastal Suffolk. In late summer the heathland bursts into colour with purple heather and yellow gorse. The clifftop walks offer panoramic views over the RSPB's Minsmere reserve and out to sea. The area is rich in wildlife including Dartford warblers, nightjars and adders, and the former coastguard cottages now house a tea room and shop.
shortDescription: Rare lowland heath with coastal views, heather and wildlife on the Suffolk coast.

name: Dyffryn Gardens
description: Dyffryn Gardens is a stunning Edwardian garden spread over 55 acres in the Vale of Glamorgan, designed by landscape architect Thomas Mawson. The gardens feature a series of themed garden rooms, an arboretum with champion trees, and a grand lawn. The restored glasshouse contains tropical and cacti collections.
shortDescription: A 55-acre Edwardian garden with themed garden rooms designed by Thomas Mawson in the Vale of Glamorgan.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Dyrham Park
description: Dyrham Park is a baroque country house built in the 1690s for William Blathwayt, Secretary of State to William III. The house is renowned for its Dutch-influenced interiors filled with Delftware, paintings, and period furniture. The 270-acre deer park offers walks with panoramic views over the surrounding countryside, while the Victorian garden features terraces and a restored greenhouse.
shortDescription: A baroque mansion with Dutch-influenced interiors set in an ancient deer park near Bath.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: East Riddlesden Hall
description: East Riddlesden Hall is a 17th-century manor house set in grounds beside the River Aire in Keighley. The dark stone building features a stunning medieval tithe barn, one of the finest in the north of England. Inside, the hall contains period furnishings and displays that bring to life the stories of its former inhabitants.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century manor house with a magnificent medieval tithe barn in Keighley.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Eastbury Manor House
description: Eastbury Manor House is an Elizabethan gentry house built around 1573 in Barking, east London, remarkably surviving amid 20th-century suburban development. The red-brick H-plan house retains original features including an unusual turret staircase, a painted chamber and a walled courtyard garden. The house has links to the Gunpowder Plot, as conspirator Lord Monteagle lived nearby.
shortDescription: Elizabethan gentry house in east London surviving from 1573 with links to the Gunpowder Plot.

name: Eggardon Hill
description: Eggardon Hill is a dramatic Iron Age hill fort crowning one of the highest points in west Dorset, with triple ramparts and deep ditches still clearly defined. The exposed hilltop offers extraordinary panoramic views stretching from the Dorset coastline to the hills of Devon and Somerset. The chalk grassland is alive with wildflowers including orchids, and the site is frequented by skylarks, buzzards, and peregrine falcons.
shortDescription: A dramatic Iron Age hill fort on a west Dorset hilltop with sweeping views to the coast and beyond.

name: Emmetts Garden
description: Emmetts Garden is a charming hillside shrub garden, one of the highest in Kent, offering stunning views across the Weald. The informal Edwardian garden features rare trees and shrubs, with spectacular displays of bluebells in spring and vibrant autumn colour. It was originally created by Frederic Lubbock in the late 19th century.
shortDescription: A hillside Edwardian shrub garden with rare trees, bluebells, and panoramic Weald views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Ennerdale
description: Ennerdale is one of the quietest and most unspoilt valleys in the Lake District, with no road running along the lake. The valley is at the heart of the Wild Ennerdale project, which is allowing the landscape to develop naturally with minimal human intervention. Ancient forest, open fell and the remote lake create a true sense of wilderness.
shortDescription: The Lake District's wildest valley, with no road and a pioneering rewilding project.

name: Erddig
description: Erddig is a remarkable country house near Wrexham that tells the story of the relationship between a wealthy family and their servants over 250 years. The house contains the most detailed collection of servants' portraits and histories of any country house in Britain. The estate includes a restored 18th-century formal garden, a walled garden and 1,200 acres of parkland.
shortDescription: A Wrexham country house uniquely telling the story of both the family and their servants over 250 years.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Eskdale and Duddon Valley
description: Eskdale and the Duddon Valley are neighbouring valleys in the western Lake District, both celebrated for their unspoilt beauty and relative quietness. Eskdale is home to the narrow-gauge Ravenglass and Eskdale Railway and the remains of a Roman bathhouse at Hardknott Fort. The Duddon Valley was a favourite of Wordsworth, who wrote a sequence of sonnets about the River Duddon.
shortDescription: Quiet western valleys with a Roman fort, miniature railway and Wordsworth's beloved River Duddon.

name: Fair Head
description: Fair Head is a dramatic headland near Ballycastle with towering dolerite cliffs rising 196 metres above the sea, making it the highest cliff face in Northern Ireland. The summit offers stunning views across to Rathlin Island, the Mull of Kintyre, and the Scottish islands. Three loughs sit at the clifftop and the area is popular with rock climbers and walkers.
shortDescription: Northern Ireland's highest cliff face with views to Rathlin Island and Scotland.

name: Farnborough Hall
description: Farnborough Hall is an elegant honey-coloured stone house set on a hilltop in Warwickshire, home to the Holbech family for over 300 years. The interior features outstanding 18th-century rococo plasterwork ceilings and the Holbech family's collection of paintings and sculpture acquired on the Grand Tour. The real treasure is the remarkable half-mile terrace walk designed in the 1740s, adorned with temples, an obelisk and a dramatic oval pavilion — one of the finest landscape terraces in England, offering sweeping views across the Warwickshire countryside.
shortDescription: Honey-stone Warwickshire hall with rococo interiors and a spectacular 18th-century terrace walk.

name: Farne Islands
description: The Farne Islands are a group of rocky islands off the Northumberland coast, home to one of Europe's most spectacular seabird colonies. Between May and July, Inner Farne is alive with puffins, Arctic terns, guillemots, razorbills and shags nesting at extraordinarily close range. The islands have a long history as a place of Christian pilgrimage — St Cuthbert lived here as a hermit in the 7th century. Grey seals breed on the islands in autumn, with over 2,000 pups born each year.
shortDescription: Spectacular Northumberland seabird colony with thousands of puffins, Arctic terns and breeding grey seals.

name: Felbrigg Hall
description: Felbrigg Hall is one of the grandest 17th-century houses in Norfolk, with a Jacobean exterior and elegant Georgian interiors. The house contains an outstanding collection of 18th-century Grand Tour paintings and an original Gothic library. The walled garden has been restored as a working kitchen garden and dovecote, while the 520-acre wooded parkland offers peaceful walks just two miles from the coast.
shortDescription: A grand 17th-century Norfolk house with Grand Tour paintings, a Gothic library, and wooded parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Fell Foot
description: Fell Foot is a beautiful Victorian park at the southern tip of Windermere, offering direct access to the lake shore. The restored boathouses provide rowing boat and kayak hire, while the sweeping lawns and mature trees make it a perfect spot for picnics. The park also features a restored Victorian garden, an adventure playground and a cafe with lake views.
shortDescription: A Victorian lakeside park at the southern tip of Windermere with boat hire and sweeping lawns.

name: Fenton House
description: Fenton House is a charming 17th-century merchant's house in Hampstead, one of the oldest and largest houses in the area. It houses the Benton Fletcher Collection of early keyboard instruments and fine porcelain, alongside paintings and Georgian furniture. The walled garden is a hidden gem with an orchard, kitchen garden, and formal terraces.
shortDescription: A 17th-century Hampstead merchant's house with early keyboard instruments and a hidden walled garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Finch Foundry
description: Finch Foundry is the last working water-powered forge in England, located in the village of Sticklepath on the northern edge of Dartmoor. Built in 1814, the foundry used three waterwheels to power massive trip hammers and a grinding stone to produce agricultural hand tools — sickles, scythes, and shovels — for farms across the South West. The original machinery still operates during demonstrations, offering a vivid glimpse of the industrial heritage that once powered rural England.
shortDescription: Last working water-powered forge in England with original 19th-century machinery on the edge of Dartmoor.

name: Flatford
description: Flatford is the heart of Constable Country, where John Constable painted some of his most famous works including The Hay Wain and Flatford Mill. The hamlet includes Flatford Mill, Bridge Cottage, and Willy Lott's House, all recognisable from Constable's paintings. The surrounding Dedham Vale Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty offers beautiful riverside walks along the Stour.
shortDescription: The heart of Constable Country with the landscapes that inspired The Hay Wain and other masterpieces.

name: Florence Court
description: Florence Court is an elegant 18th-century house set against the backdrop of Cuilcagh Mountain in County Fermanagh. The house, home to the Cole family (Earls of Enniskillen), features fine rococo plasterwork and period furnishings. The grounds include a walled garden, pleasure grounds, and the parent tree of all Irish yew trees, propagated worldwide since 1767.
shortDescription: An elegant 18th-century Fermanagh mansion with fine rococo plasterwork and the original Irish yew tree.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Fontmell and Melbury Downs
description: Fontmell and Melbury Downs are sweeping chalk downlands on the edge of Cranborne Chase, forming one of the finest stretches of unimproved chalk grassland in Dorset. The steep escarpment and rolling hilltops support a remarkable diversity of wildflowers, butterflies — including chalkhill blue and Adonis blue — and ground-nesting birds. Circular walks offer panoramic views across the Blackmore Vale and surrounding Dorset countryside.
shortDescription: Pristine chalk downlands rich in rare butterflies and wildflowers with panoramic Blackmore Vale views.

name: Formby
description: Formby is a stunning stretch of the Sefton Coast featuring wide sandy beaches, towering sand dunes and pine woodlands that are home to one of England's few remaining red squirrel populations. The asparagus fields behind the dunes are a reminder of the area's Victorian market gardening heritage. At low tide, prehistoric human and animal footprints dating back 5,000 years are occasionally revealed on the beach, offering a unique window into the past.
shortDescription: Red squirrel reserve and pine woodland behind wide sandy beaches on the Sefton Coast.

name: Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal
description: Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site near Ripon, combining the spectacular ruins of a 12th-century Cistercian monastery with one of the finest surviving Georgian water gardens in England. The estate also includes a medieval deer park, St Mary's Church designed by William Burges, and an Elizabethan mansion. It is one of the National Trust's most visited properties.
shortDescription: UNESCO World Heritage Site combining Cistercian abbey ruins with a Georgian water garden near Ripon.

name: Fyne Court
description: Fyne Court is a nature reserve and garden in the Quantock Hills, once the estate of Andrew Crosse, the Victorian gentleman scientist known as the 'thunder and lightning man' for his electrical experiments. The house was destroyed by fire in 1894, but the grounds survive with specimen trees, a walled garden and woodland walks. The estate now serves as a base for exploring the Quantock Hills, England's first Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.
shortDescription: Quantock Hills garden and nature reserve, former estate of the Victorian electrical experimenter Andrew Crosse.

name: Gawthorpe Hall
description: Gawthorpe Hall is an Elizabethan gem set on the banks of the River Calder near Burnley, restored in the 1850s by Sir Charles Barry of Houses of Parliament fame. The house contains the Rachel Kay-Shuttleworth textile collection, one of the most important in Europe. The grounds include a formal parterre garden and woodland walks along the river.
shortDescription: Elizabethan hall near Burnley with Barry interiors and a world-class textile collection.

name: George Inn
description: The George Inn in Southwark is London's last surviving galleried coaching inn, dating from the 17th century and rebuilt after the Southwark fire of 1676. The timber-framed inn with its open galleries overlooks a cobbled courtyard and was mentioned by Dickens in Little Dorrit. Now a working pub managed by the National Trust, it retains its historic character and atmospheric old bars.
shortDescription: London's last galleried coaching inn, a 17th-century Southwark pub mentioned by Dickens.

name: George Stephenson's Birthplace
description: George Stephenson's Birthplace is the modest stone cottage in Wylam, Northumberland, where the father of the railways was born in 1781. The single-room tenement, shared by several families, illustrates the humble origins of the engineer who built the Rocket locomotive and pioneered the world's first public railway. The cottage contains displays on Stephenson's life and the early history of rail travel.
shortDescription: Humble Wylam cottage where the father of the railways George Stephenson was born in 1781.

name: Giant's Causeway
description: Giant's Causeway is a UNESCO World Heritage Site on the north Antrim coast, famous for its 40,000 interlocking basalt columns formed by volcanic activity 60 million years ago. Legend attributes the columns to the giant Finn McCool who built a causeway to Scotland. The visitor centre tells the geological and mythological story of this extraordinary landscape.
shortDescription: A UNESCO World Heritage Site of 40,000 volcanic basalt columns steeped in the legend of Finn McCool.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Gibside
description: Gibside is a Georgian landscape garden and forest estate in the Derwent Valley near Gateshead. Once the ancestral home of the Bowes-Lyon family, the estate features the stunning Palladian Chapel, a grand Column of Liberty and miles of riverside and woodland walks. The ruined Gibside Hall stands as a reminder of the estate's former grandeur.
shortDescription: A Georgian forest estate in the Derwent Valley with a Palladian chapel and Bowes-Lyon heritage.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Glastonbury Tor
description: Glastonbury Tor is an iconic conical hill rising 158 metres above the Somerset Levels, crowned by the roofless tower of the medieval St Michael's Church. Steeped in legend and myth — linked to King Arthur, the Holy Grail, and the Isle of Avalon — the Tor has been a place of spiritual significance for thousands of years. The summit offers breathtaking 360-degree views across Somerset to the Mendip Hills, Quantocks, and beyond.
shortDescription: An iconic hilltop tower steeped in Arthurian legend with panoramic views across the Somerset Levels.

name: Glendurgan Garden
description: Glendurgan Garden is a subtropical valley garden running down to the tiny fishing hamlet of Durgan on the Helford River. Created in the 1820s by the Fox family, the garden features a famous laurel maze, giant tree ferns and a stunning collection of camellias and magnolias. Three valleys of exotic planting create sheltered microclimates perfect for tender species.
shortDescription: Subtropical valley garden with a famous laurel maze tumbling down to the Helford River.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Glenoe Waterfall
description: Glenoe Waterfall is a picturesque waterfall near the village of Gleno in County Antrim, cascading down a rocky gorge surrounded by mature woodland. A short walk from the village leads to the falls, which are particularly impressive after heavy rainfall. The surrounding countryside offers views across to the Scottish coast and is rich in wildflowers and birdlife.
shortDescription: A picturesque waterfall cascading through a wooded gorge near Gleno with views to Scotland.

name: Goddards House and Garden
description: Goddards is an Arts and Crafts house in York designed by Walter Brierley, the leading architect of Edwardian Yorkshire, and built in 1927 as a home for retired Terry's chocolate factory workers. The house features Brierley's characteristic attention to craftsmanship and materials, with handsome interiors and a fine garden designed by George Dillistone. It later served as the headquarters of the National Trust in Yorkshire.
shortDescription: Arts and Crafts house in York designed by Walter Brierley, originally a home for Terry's chocolate workers.

name: Godolphin
description: Godolphin is a romantic former Tudor and Stuart estate near Helston, once home to the powerful Godolphin family who made their fortune from local tin mining. The intimate house has medieval origins and retains rare Elizabethan features. The garden, once lost to nature, has been carefully reclaimed and includes the King's Garden and the celebrated Side Garden.
shortDescription: Romantic Tudor estate near Helston with reclaimed gardens and Cornish mining heritage.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Godrevy
description: Godrevy is a stunning headland on the north Cornwall coast, famous for its lighthouse which inspired Virginia Woolf's novel 'To the Lighthouse'. The area offers beautiful sandy beaches, dramatic clifftop walks, and is home to a colony of grey seals.
shortDescription: Beautiful headland with lighthouse, sandy beaches and seal colony on the north Cornwall coast.

name: Golden Cap
description: Golden Cap is the highest point on the south coast of England at 191 metres, named for the distinctive golden-orange sandstone cap rock that crowns its summit. Part of the Jurassic Coast World Heritage Site, the cliffs reveal 185 million years of geological history and are rich in fossils. The surrounding estate includes wildflower meadows, ancient woodlands, hidden valleys, and secluded beaches accessible by footpath.
shortDescription: The highest cliff on England's south coast, a golden-topped landmark on the Jurassic Coast.

name: Grantham House
description: Grantham House is an elegant town house dating from the 14th century, set in peaceful walled gardens beside the River Witham. The house has a rich history spanning six centuries and its gardens offer a tranquil retreat in the heart of Grantham.
shortDescription: An elegant 14th-century town house with walled gardens beside the River Witham.

name: Gray's Printing Press
description: Gray's Printing Press in Strabane is a small museum preserving an 18th-century print shop where John Dunlap, printer of the American Declaration of Independence, and James Wilson, grandfather of President Woodrow Wilson, learned their trade. The building houses original hand presses and typesetting equipment. It tells the remarkable story of Strabane's printing heritage and its links to America.
shortDescription: An 18th-century print shop in Strabane where the printer of the American Declaration of Independence learned his trade.

name: Great Chalfield Manor
description: Great Chalfield Manor is a moated 15th-century manor house near Melksham, one of the finest examples of late medieval domestic architecture in England. The house was built around 1480 by Thomas Tropenell, a wealthy cloth merchant, and features an impressive great hall with an oriel window, a screens passage and a remarkable set of peeping masks through which the lord could observe his guests. The Arts and Crafts garden, created by Major Robert Fuller in the early 20th century, complements the medieval architecture with rose gardens, topiary and a gazebo overlooking the moat.
shortDescription: Moated 15th-century manor near Melksham with medieval great hall, peeping masks and an Arts and Crafts garden.

name: Greenway
description: Greenway is the beloved holiday home of the crime writer Agatha Christie, set in a romantic woodland garden on the banks of the River Dart. The house contains Christie's personal collections, books, archaeological finds and her drawing room where she entertained guests. The surrounding 30-acre garden descends to a boathouse on the river and includes rare champion trees.
shortDescription: Agatha Christie's riverside holiday home on the Dart with woodland gardens and personal collections.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Greys Court
description: Greys Court is a picturesque Tudor mansion set amid medieval ruins, including a fortified tower and the remains of a 14th-century courtyard. The intimate walled gardens include a rose garden, kitchen garden, and the Archbishop's Maze, inspired by the journey of reconciliation. The surrounding estate offers walks through ancient woodland and Chiltern countryside.
shortDescription: A picturesque Tudor mansion with medieval ruins, a walled garden, and the Archbishop's Maze.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Gunby Hall
description: Gunby Hall is a red-brick William and Mary house built in 1700, set in the gentle Lincolnshire Wolds countryside near Spilsby. Alfred Tennyson called it 'a haunt of ancient peace', and the house retains an atmosphere of quiet domesticity with its panelled rooms, family portraits and well-stocked library. The walled garden is one of the finest in the county, with herbaceous borders, old-fashioned roses, a Victorian glasshouse and productive fruit and vegetable plots. The surrounding parkland features ancient sweet chestnut trees and peaceful walks.
shortDescription: William and Mary house in the Lincolnshire Wolds, Tennyson's 'haunt of ancient peace' with a fine walled garden.

name: Hadrian's Wall and Housesteads Fort
description: The National Trust cares for some of the most dramatic sections of Hadrian's Wall where it rides the crags of the Whin Sill in Northumberland. This UNESCO World Heritage Site stretches across wild, open countryside with sweeping views in every direction. The fort at Housesteads is managed by English Heritage, while the surrounding landscape of crags, loughs and farmland is in National Trust care.
shortDescription: Dramatic sections of the Roman frontier wall riding the Northumberland crags, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Ham House and Garden
description: Ham House is one of Europe's best-preserved 17th-century houses, built in 1610 and lavishly expanded by the Duke and Duchess of Lauderdale. The house retains much of its original furniture, textiles, and paintings, including many Stuart-era pieces. The formal garden has been restored to its 17th-century design with a cherry garden, wilderness, and kitchen garden.
shortDescription: One of Europe's best-preserved Stuart-era houses with original furnishings and a restored 17th-century garden.

name: Hambledon Hill
description: Hambledon Hill is a prominent chalk hill crowned by a massive Neolithic causewayed enclosure and Iron Age hill fort, with earthworks spanning over 5,000 years of human activity. The site saw dramatic conflict during the English Civil War when Clubmen — local people opposing both sides — were routed by Oliver Cromwell's forces. The steep slopes support species-rich chalk grassland with orchids, butterflies, and commanding views over the Blackmore Vale.
shortDescription: A Neolithic and Iron Age hilltop site with Civil War history and species-rich chalk grasslands.

name: Hanbury Hall
description: Hanbury Hall is a William and Mary-style red-brick house built in 1701 for the wealthy lawyer Thomas Vernon. The highlight is the stunning painted staircase and ceiling by Sir James Thornhill, rivalling his work at St Paul's Cathedral and Greenwich. The restored 18th-century formal gardens include a sunken parterre, fruit garden, bowling green, and an ice house.
shortDescription: A Queen Anne mansion famous for its Thornhill-painted staircase and restored 18th-century gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardcastle Crags
description: Hardcastle Crags is a deeply wooded Pennine valley near Hebden Bridge, a haven of tumbling streams, mossy boulders and ancient oak woodland. At the heart of the valley stands Gibson Mill, a former cotton mill now powered entirely by renewable energy and serving as an interactive exhibition about sustainable living. The crags themselves are outcrops of millstone grit scattered through the woodland, and the valley supports a rich variety of wildlife including dippers, kingfishers and rare moths.
shortDescription: Wooded Pennine valley near Hebden Bridge with Gibson Mill, tumbling streams and millstone grit crags.

name: Hardwick Hall
description: Hardwick Hall is an Elizabethan masterpiece built in the 1590s by Bess of Hardwick, one of the richest women in England. Famous for its vast windows — inspiring the rhyme 'Hardwick Hall, more glass than wall' — the house contains outstanding tapestries and embroideries. The estate includes formal gardens, an Elizabethan herb garden, and the ruins of the adjacent Old Hall.
shortDescription: A spectacular Elizabethan prodigy house famous for its vast windows and outstanding tapestries.

name: Hardy Monument
description: The Hardy Monument is a tall octagonal tower erected in 1844 on Black Down hill to commemorate Vice-Admiral Sir Thomas Masterman Hardy, flag captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar. Standing at 22 metres tall on one of the highest points in south Dorset, it commands exceptional panoramic views over the coast, Portland, Chesil Beach, and the Dorset countryside. The surrounding heathland is managed for wildlife and offers bracing walks.
shortDescription: A hilltop monument to Nelson's flag captain at Trafalgar, commanding sweeping Dorset coastal views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardy's Cottage
description: Hardy's Cottage is the small cob and thatch cottage in Higher Bockhampton where the novelist and poet Thomas Hardy was born in 1840 and wrote his early novels including 'Under the Greenwood Tree' and 'Far from the Madding Crowd.' The cottage was built by Hardy's great-grandfather and retains its modest rural character. The surrounding woodland and heath inspired many settings in Hardy's Wessex novels.
shortDescription: The thatched birthplace of Thomas Hardy, where he wrote his first published novels.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hardy's House, Max Gate
description: Max Gate is the Victorian house designed by Thomas Hardy himself in 1885, where he lived until his death in 1928 and wrote many of his greatest works including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles' and 'Jude the Obscure.' The modest but comfortable house reflects Hardy's architectural training and personal taste. The garden, which Hardy planted with extensive tree cover for privacy, has been replanted with species he would have known.
shortDescription: The house Thomas Hardy designed for himself, where he wrote his greatest novels and poetry.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hare Hill
description: Hare Hill is an intimate woodland garden near Macclesfield, celebrated for its spectacular display of rhododendrons and azaleas in late spring. A walled garden at the heart of the property provides a sheltered haven with year-round interest. Surrounding parkland and woodland offer peaceful walks with views towards the Peak District.
shortDescription: Intimate woodland garden near Macclesfield famed for its spectacular rhododendron displays.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Additional Family e.g. 1 adult and 2 children

name: Haresfield Beacon
description: Haresfield Beacon is a prominent viewpoint on the Cotswold escarpment offering sweeping views across the Severn Vale to the Forest of Dean and Wales beyond. The site includes an Iron Age hill fort and is surrounded by wildflower-rich grassland and ancient woodland.
shortDescription: A dramatic Cotswold viewpoint with Iron Age hill fort and panoramic Severn Vale views.

name: Hatchlands Park
description: Hatchlands Park is a Georgian country house built in the 1750s with an interior by Robert Adam, his earliest known decorative scheme. The house contains the Cobbe Collection, the world's largest collection of keyboard instruments associated with famous composers. The 430-acre park offers varied walks through ancient woodland and open parkland.
shortDescription: A Georgian house with Robert Adam interiors, the world's largest keyboard instrument collection, and 430 acres of parkland.

name: Hatfield Forest
description: Hatfield Forest is one of the finest surviving examples of a medieval royal hunting forest in England, remarkably intact after 1,000 years. The forest contains ancient coppiced woodland, wood pasture, a lake created in the 18th century and an ornamental Shell House. It is home to great crested newts, 15 species of bat, purple emperor butterflies and over 1,000 species of beetle. The wide green rides and open plains still reflect its original medieval structure.
shortDescription: A thousand-year-old royal hunting forest with ancient woodland, a lake and an ornamental Shell House.

name: Hawford Dovecote
description: Hawford Dovecote is a half-timbered 16th-century dovecote, one of the finest surviving examples in Worcestershire. The octagonal structure retains many of its original nesting boxes and provides a charming example of Tudor agricultural architecture.
shortDescription: A fine half-timbered 16th-century dovecote in Worcestershire.

name: Heddon Valley
description: Heddon Valley is a dramatic wooded valley on the Exmoor coast where the River Heddon flows through ancient oak woodland to the sea at Heddon's Mouth. The valley offers wonderful walks through one of the largest areas of native oak woodland in the South West.
shortDescription: A dramatic wooded valley on the Exmoor coast with ancient oak woodland and coastal walks.

name: Heysham Head
description: Heysham Head is a rocky promontory on the Lancashire coast with a remarkable collection of early Christian remains. The ruins of St Patrick's Chapel and the famous rock-cut graves, carved into the cliff edge, date back over a thousand years.
shortDescription: A rocky headland with ancient rock-cut graves and early Christian ruins on the Lancashire coast.

name: Hezlett House
description: Hezlett House is a thatched 17th-century cottage near Castlerock in County Londonderry, one of the oldest surviving buildings in Northern Ireland. Built around 1690 using cruck-truss construction, the house was home to the Hezlett family for generations. Its simple whitewashed exterior and period furnishings offer a glimpse into centuries of rural domestic life.
shortDescription: A rare thatched 17th-century cruck-truss cottage near Castlerock, one of Northern Ireland's oldest buildings.

name: Hidcote
description: Hidcote is one of England's great Arts and Crafts gardens, created in the early 20th century by the American-born horticulturalist Lawrence Johnston. The garden is a masterful sequence of outdoor 'rooms,' each with a distinct character, separated by tall hedges and walls. Rare and unusual plants from Johnston's global expeditions fill the borders, and the design has influenced gardens worldwide.
shortDescription: One of England's most influential Arts and Crafts gardens, designed as a series of outdoor rooms.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hill Top
description: Hill Top is the 17th-century farmhouse where Beatrix Potter wrote and illustrated many of her beloved children's stories. The house is preserved exactly as she left it, filled with her furniture, china and personal belongings. The cottage garden provided inspiration for many of her illustrations and remains a highlight of any visit to the Lake District.
shortDescription: Beatrix Potter's beloved 17th-century farmhouse, preserved as she left it in Near Sawrey.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Hindhead Common
description: Hindhead Common and the Devil's Punch Bowl form one of the largest areas of open heath in Surrey, a dramatic landscape of purple heather, golden gorse and ancient Scots pine. The Devil's Punch Bowl is a vast natural amphitheatre carved by springs during the last Ice Age, offering spectacular walking routes around its rim and down to the valley floor. Since the opening of the Hindhead Tunnel, the old A3 road has been removed and the common is now reunited for the first time in over a century.
shortDescription: Dramatic heathland and the Devil's Punch Bowl, a vast natural amphitheatre in the Surrey Hills.

name: Hinton Ampner
description: Hinton Ampner is an elegant country house remodelled in the mid-20th century by Ralph Dutton, the last Lord Sherborne. It showcases his superb collection of English furniture, Italian paintings, and hard-stone ornaments. The terraced garden, designed by Dutton himself, is considered one of the great gardens of the 20th century with sweeping views over the Hampshire countryside.
shortDescription: An elegant country house with fine collections and a celebrated 20th-century terraced garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Historic Steam Yacht Gondola
description: The Steam Yacht Gondola is a beautifully restored Victorian steam-powered vessel that has been sailing on Coniston Water since 1859. Rebuilt by the National Trust in the 1980s, she carries passengers on scenic cruises past Brantwood (John Ruskin's home) and through stunning mountain scenery. The opulently upholstered saloons evoke the elegance of Victorian lake travel.
shortDescription: A restored Victorian steam yacht offering scenic cruises on Coniston Water since 1859.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Hive Beach
description: Hive Beach is a dramatic shingle beach at Burton Bradstock on the Jurassic Coast, backed by towering golden sandstone cliffs that glow in the sunlight. The beach is popular for swimming, fossil hunting, and fishing, with views east along Chesil Beach towards Portland. The clifftop café is well known locally, and coastal footpaths lead to neighbouring bays and the Golden Cap estate.
shortDescription: A dramatic Jurassic Coast shingle beach beneath golden sandstone cliffs at Burton Bradstock.

name: Hod Hill
description: Hod Hill is one of the largest Iron Age hillforts in Britain, with dramatic ramparts enclosing over 50 acres of Dorset downland. The Romans later built a fort in one corner after conquering the site in AD 43. Today the hill offers panoramic views across the Stour Valley and is rich in wildflowers and butterflies.
shortDescription: Massive Iron Age hillfort with Roman remains and sweeping views across the Stour Valley.

name: Holnicote Estate
description: The Holnicote Estate is a 12,500-acre landscape on the northern slopes of Exmoor encompassing moorland, woodland, farmland and coast. The estate includes the picturesque villages of Selworthy and Allerford, the wooded combes of Horner Wood and access to Dunkery Beacon, the highest point on Exmoor. The ancient oak woodland of Horner Wood is one of the largest remaining fragments of the original Exmoor forest.
shortDescription: 12,500-acre Exmoor estate with picturesque villages, ancient Horner Wood and access to Dunkery Beacon.

name: Horsey Windpump
description: Horsey Windpump is a restored drainage windpump on the edge of Horsey Mere in the Norfolk Broads, built in 1912 to drain the surrounding marshes for farming. The four-storey windpump has been restored to working condition and visitors can climb to the top for panoramic views across Horsey Mere and the Broads landscape. The surrounding estate is one of the best places in England to see grey seals, with hundreds hauling out on Horsey beach each winter to pup, making it one of the largest mainland seal colonies in the country.
shortDescription: Restored Norfolk Broads windpump with panoramic views and one of England's largest grey seal colonies.

name: Horton Court
description: Horton Court is a Cotswold manor house with a remarkable 12th-century Norman hall, one of the oldest domestic interiors in England. The house was later remodelled in the 16th century by William Knight, a diplomat for Henry VIII, who added an Italianate loggia inspired by his travels in Rome. The detached ambulatory in the garden is a rare example of early Renaissance architecture in England.
shortDescription: Cotswold manor with a 12th-century Norman hall and rare 16th-century Italianate loggia.

name: Houghton Mill
description: Houghton Mill is the last remaining working watermill on the Great Ouse, set on an island in the river near St Ives in Cambridgeshire. The present mill dates from the 17th century, though milling has taken place on this site since the 10th century as recorded in the Domesday Book. On milling days, visitors can watch the traditional millstones grinding organic wheat into flour, which is sold in the mill shop. The surrounding Waterclose Meadows provide tranquil riverside walks and picnic spots.
shortDescription: Last working watermill on the Great Ouse, grinding flour since the 10th century on a Cambridgeshire river island.

name: Hughenden Manor
description: Hughenden is a red brick Victorian manor set in the Chiltern Hills, the former country home of Prime Minister Benjamin Disraeli. The house reveals Disraeli's colourful personal life and political career, while the grounds concealed a top-secret Second World War operation codenamed 'Hillside'. The estate spans over 600 acres of woodland and farmland, with formal Italianate gardens featuring a Victorian parterre and classical statuary, pleasure grounds, an apple orchard and a walled garden.
shortDescription: Victorian manor in the Chilterns, former home of PM Benjamin Disraeli, with secret WWII history and Italianate gardens.

name: Ickworth
description: Ickworth is one of the most unusual country houses in England, with an enormous Italianate rotunda at its centre flanked by curving wings. Built by the eccentric 4th Earl of Bristol in the late 18th century, the house contains outstanding collections of Georgian silver, Regency furniture, and Old Master paintings. The 1,800-acre parkland, partly designed by Capability Brown, includes an Italianate garden, vineyard walk, and woodland play area.
shortDescription: An extraordinary Italianate rotunda house with Georgian silver and 1,800 acres of Capability Brown parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Ightham Mote
description: Ightham Mote is a medieval moated manor house dating from around 1320, one of the oldest and most complete in England. The house spans seven centuries of architectural history, from the Great Hall to the Tudor chapel with its painted ceiling. The tranquil garden and surrounding countryside walks add to its charm.
shortDescription: A beautifully preserved medieval moated manor house spanning seven centuries of architectural history.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Ilam Park
description: Ilam Park is a beautiful parkland estate in the Manifold Valley on the southern edge of the Peak District, forming the gateway to the dramatic limestone dale of Dovedale. The park surrounds Ilam Hall, a Gothic Revival mansion partly demolished in the 1930s but now serving as a youth hostel. The grounds feature the 'Paradise Walk' along the River Manifold, which famously disappears underground before re-emerging at 'boil holes' in the park. Dovedale itself, with its famous stepping stones and towering limestone pinnacles, is one of the Peak District's most celebrated landscapes.
shortDescription: Peak District parkland at the gateway to Dovedale, with riverside walks and the famous stepping stones.

name: Islandmagee
description: Islandmagee is a peninsula on the east Antrim coast between Larne Lough and the Irish Sea, featuring dramatic basalt cliffs known as the Gobbins. The National Trust protects stretches of this rugged coastline, which shelters rock pools, seabird colonies, and wildflower-rich grassland. Brown's Bay offers safe swimming and views across to the Scottish coast.
shortDescription: A rugged east Antrim peninsula with dramatic basalt cliffs, rock pools, and coastal grassland.

name: Kedleston Hall
description: Kedleston Hall is a magnificent Palladian mansion designed by Robert Adam in the 1760s for the Curzon family. The grand marble hall and state rooms contain outstanding collections of paintings, furniture, and Old Master drawings. The 800-acre landscaped park features a serpentine lake, a medieval church, and long walks through ancient woodland and pleasure grounds.
shortDescription: A grand Palladian mansion by Robert Adam set in 800 acres of sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Keld Chapel
description: Keld Chapel is a tiny pre-Reformation chapel tucked away in the hamlet of Keld near Shap. Dating from the early 16th century, it is one of very few surviving buildings of its kind in the region. The simple stone building has been sensitively maintained and is freely accessible to visitors year-round.
shortDescription: A rare pre-Reformation chapel from the early 1500s, tucked away in a quiet hamlet near Shap.

name: Killerton
description: Killerton is an 18th-century house set in extensive hillside gardens and a sweeping Devon estate near Exeter. The house is known for its nationally important fashion collection, displayed in changing exhibitions throughout the year. The gardens are celebrated for their champion trees, rhododendrons, and herbaceous borders, while the wider estate includes farmland, orchards and woodland walks.
shortDescription: Devon hillside estate near Exeter with champion trees and a celebrated fashion collection.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: King Alfred's Tower
description: King Alfred's Tower is a 160-foot triangular brick folly on the Stourhead estate, designed by Henry Flitcroft and completed in 1772 to commemorate King Alfred the Great's rallying of the Saxons against the Danes in 878 AD. The tower stands at the point where the counties of Wiltshire, Somerset and Dorset meet, and climbing its 205 steps rewards visitors with views extending across three counties. It marks the spot where Alfred is said to have raised his standard before the Battle of Edington.
shortDescription: 160-foot triangular folly on the Stourhead estate commemorating King Alfred the Great, with three-county views.

name: Kingston Lacy
description: Kingston Lacy is a grand 17th-century country house built for the Bankes family after they lost Corfe Castle during the English Civil War. The lavish interiors contain an outstanding art collection, including works by Rubens, Titian, and Van Dyck, as well as an Egyptian obelisk in the grounds. The 8,500-acre estate includes ancient woodland, Iron Age hill forts, and the famous Badbury Rings.
shortDescription: A grand country house with masterpieces by Rubens and Titian, set in an 8,500-acre estate.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Kinver Edge
description: Kinver Edge is a dramatic sandstone ridge on the Staffordshire-Worcestershire border, famous for its extraordinary rock houses carved into the soft red sandstone. People lived in these rock-cut dwellings from the 17th century until the 1960s, and the National Trust has restored several to show different periods of their history. The heathland above offers panoramic views across the Midlands, while the Holy Austin Rock houses are the last troglodyte dwellings in England.
shortDescription: Sandstone ridge with the last troglodyte rock houses in England and panoramic Midlands views.

name: Knightshayes
description: Knightshayes is a Victorian Gothic Revival house near Tiverton designed by William Burges, one of the most original architects of the 19th century. The richly decorated interiors include painted ceilings and elaborate carvings. The surrounding garden is one of the finest in Devon, with rare shrubs, a walled kitchen garden, a topiary fox hunt, and sweeping parkland views.
shortDescription: Gothic Revival house near Tiverton with Burges interiors and one of Devon's finest gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Knole
description: Knole is one of the largest and most important houses in England, set within a medieval deer park. Dating from the 15th century, the house has 365 rooms and contains outstanding collections of furniture, textiles, and paintings. Virginia Woolf immortalised it in her novel Orlando.
shortDescription: A vast 15th-century house with 365 rooms, priceless collections, and a medieval deer park.

name: Kynance Cove
description: Kynance Cove is a stunning beach on the Lizard Peninsula, celebrated for its turquoise waters, white sand and dramatic serpentine rock stacks. The cove has inspired artists and writers since the Victorian era and is considered one of the most beautiful beaches in Britain. At low tide, visitors can explore caves and islands formed from the colourful serpentine stone.
shortDescription: Spectacular Lizard Peninsula beach with turquoise waters, serpentine rock stacks and hidden caves.

name: Lacock Abbey
description: Lacock Abbey is a medieval nunnery converted into a country house after the dissolution, set in the picturesque village of Lacock. The house is celebrated as the birthplace of photography, where William Henry Fox Talbot created his first photographic negative in 1835. The abbey retains its medieval cloisters, and the museum tells the story of Talbot's pioneering experiments.
shortDescription: Medieval abbey and birthplace of photography, set in the picturesque village of Lacock.

name: Lamb House
description: Lamb House is a handsome Georgian house on a cobbled street in the hilltop town of Rye, famous as the home of the novelist Henry James from 1898 to 1916. James wrote some of his greatest works here, including The Wings of the Dove and The Golden Bowl. The house was later home to E.F. Benson, author of the Mapp and Lucia novels, who used the house as the model for Miss Mapp's home. The walled garden provides a peaceful retreat with herbaceous borders and fine views.
shortDescription: Georgian house in Rye, home to novelists Henry James and E.F. Benson, with a walled garden.

name: Lambert's and Coney's Castle
description: Lambert's Castle and Coney's Castle are neighbouring Iron Age hillforts set in the rolling hills of west Dorset near the Devon border. The sites are cloaked in woodland and heathland, providing habitat for rare butterflies and birds. Superb views extend south to the Jurassic Coast and north across the Marshwood Vale.
shortDescription: Twin Iron Age hillforts in west Dorset with heathland, woodland and far-reaching coastal views.

name: Lanhydrock
description: Lanhydrock is a magnificent late Victorian country house set in extensive grounds above the River Fowey near Bodmin. The house, largely rebuilt after a fire in 1881, offers a vivid picture of life in a wealthy Victorian household from the grand Long Gallery to the sprawling kitchens. The surrounding estate includes formal gardens, ancient parkland and woodland walks.
shortDescription: Grand Victorian country house near Bodmin with extensive gardens and riverside parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
One adult and up to 3 children

name: Lavenham Guildhall
description: Lavenham Guildhall is a stunning timber-framed Tudor building dating from the 1520s, originally the meeting hall of the Guild of Corpus Christi. The building has served as a town hall, prison, workhouse, and almshouse over the centuries. Today it houses exhibitions on local history, the medieval wool trade, and farming, set in one of England's best-preserved medieval villages.
shortDescription: A magnificent timber-framed Tudor guildhall in one of England's finest medieval villages.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family One Adult (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Leith Hill
description: Leith Hill is the highest point in south-east England at 294 metres, crowned by an 18th-century Gothic tower built by Richard Hull of Leith Hill Place. Climbing the tower takes visitors above 1,000 feet with far-reaching views across the Weald to the South Downs and, on clear days, to the English Channel. The surrounding estate includes ancient woodland, rhododendron walks, and Leith Hill Place, a house with connections to composer Ralph Vaughan Williams who grew up here.
shortDescription: Highest point in south-east England with a Gothic tower, sweeping views and woodland walks.

name: Levant Mine and Beam Engine
description: Levant Mine is a restored 19th-century tin and copper mine perched on dramatic cliffs near St Just in Cornwall's far west. The site houses the oldest beam engine in Cornwall, still powered by steam on regular steaming days. The mine is part of the Cornwall and West Devon Mining Landscape UNESCO World Heritage Site and offers spectacular Atlantic views.
shortDescription: Restored clifftop tin mine with the oldest working beam engine in Cornwall.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Lewesdon Hill
description: Lewesdon Hill is the highest point in Dorset at 279 metres, crowned by ancient beech trees and surrounded by species-rich grassland. A short but steep walk leads to the summit, which offers panoramic views in every direction. The hill is a haven for wildlife including dormice and a wide range of woodland birds.
shortDescription: Dorset's highest hill, offering panoramic views from a beech-crowned summit.

name: Lindisfarne Castle
description: Lindisfarne Castle stands on a dramatic rocky promontory on Holy Island, accessible only by a tidal causeway from the Northumberland coast. Originally a Tudor fort, it was converted into a private home by architect Edwin Lutyens in 1903. The castle features a small but charming walled garden designed by Gertrude Jekyll and offers spectacular views across the sea and coastline.
shortDescription: A dramatic Tudor-fort-turned-Lutyens-home on Holy Island, reached by tidal causeway.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Lisnabreeny and Cregagh Glen
description: Lisnabreeny and Cregagh Glen is a green corridor of woodland and rath on the edge of east Belfast, offering walking trails through a wooded glen and open hillside with views over the city. The rath (ring fort) dates from the early Christian period and is a scheduled monument. The glen's stream, wildflowers, and birdsong make it a valued urban nature escape.
shortDescription: A wooded glen and ancient rath on the edge of east Belfast with panoramic city views.

name: Little Moreton Hall
description: Little Moreton Hall is one of the finest examples of a timber-framed moated manor house in England, dating from the mid-15th century. The wonky black-and-white building, with its elaborate carved gables and long gallery, appears to defy gravity. A knot garden based on a 17th-century design and a small border garden surround the moat.
shortDescription: Iconic wonky timber-framed moated manor house, one of England's finest medieval buildings.

name: Llanerchaeron
description: Llanerchaeron is a self-sufficient 18th-century Welsh gentry estate designed by John Nash, set in the Aeron valley. The property includes a villa, walled gardens, a home farm with original outbuildings, and a lake. It offers a rare surviving example of the type of estate that was once common across Wales.
shortDescription: A John Nash-designed 18th-century Welsh gentry estate with walled gardens and a working home farm.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Lodge Park and Sherborne Estate
description: Lodge Park is a unique 17th-century grandstand built in 1634 by John 'Crump' Dutton for the sport of deer coursing, making it the earliest surviving example of such a building in England. The Sherborne Estate surrounding it encompasses rolling Cotswold parkland with ancient woodland, water meadows and the village of Sherborne. The restored grandstand offers views across the designed landscape where coursing once took place.
shortDescription: England's oldest surviving deer-coursing grandstand set in rolling Cotswold parkland and ancient woodland.

name: Longshaw Estate
description: Longshaw Estate is a 1,600-acre expanse of moorland, woodland and parkland in the Peak District, stretching from the ancient oak woods of Padley Gorge to the gritstone edges of Burbage and the high moorland beyond. The estate centres on Longshaw Lodge, a former shooting lodge built for the Duke of Rutland in the 1820s. The diverse landscape supports a rich variety of wildlife including mountain hares, ring ouzels and adders, while the ancient lead mining remains and millstone quarries tell the story of centuries of human industry on the moors.
shortDescription: 1,600-acre Peak District estate with Padley Gorge, gritstone edges, moorland walks and rich wildlife.

name: Lundy
description: Lundy is a remote granite island three miles long in the Bristol Channel, twelve miles off the Devon coast, owned by the National Trust and managed by the Landmark Trust. The island's dramatic cliffs and clear waters form England's first Marine Conservation Zone, home to grey seals, puffins (from which the island takes its Norse name) and basking sharks. On land, the island supports wild Soay sheep, sika deer and the unique Lundy cabbage, found nowhere else on Earth. Visitors can reach the island by ferry from Ilfracombe or Bideford and explore its lighthouses, castle ruins and wild coastal scenery.
shortDescription: Remote granite island in the Bristol Channel with puffins, seals and England's first Marine Conservation Zone.

name: Lydford Gorge
description: Lydford Gorge is the deepest river gorge in the South West, carved out by the River Lyd over thousands of years on the edge of Dartmoor. The dramatic one-and-a-half-mile trail follows the river through ancient oak woodland past the thundering 30-metre White Lady Waterfall and the swirling whirlpools of the Devil's Cauldron. The gorge is a haven for wildlife including dippers, grey wagtails and over 40 species of lichen.
shortDescription: Devon's deepest gorge with the White Lady Waterfall, Devil's Cauldron and ancient woodland trails.

name: Lyme
description: Lyme is a grand Elizabethan mansion set in a medieval deer park on the edge of the Peak District near Stockport. Home to the Legh family for over 600 years, the house contains Mortlake tapestries and an important collection of English clocks. The park's ancient herd of red deer, Edwardian rose garden, and moorland walks make it a major visitor attraction.
shortDescription: Elizabethan mansion in a medieval deer park on the edge of the Peak District.

name: Lytes Cary Manor
description: Lytes Cary Manor is a charming medieval manor house that was home to the Lyte family for 500 years, including the Elizabethan botanist Henry Lyte who wrote the influential herbal 'Niewe Herball.' The intimate rooms contain period furnishings and a collection of Lyte's botanical works. The Arts and Crafts-style gardens, created in the 20th century, feature herbaceous borders, topiary, and a tranquil orchard.
shortDescription: A medieval manor house linked to the Elizabethan botanist Henry Lyte, with Arts and Crafts gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Lyveden
description: Lyveden is an unfinished Elizabethan lodge built by Sir Thomas Tresham to express his Catholic faith through symbolic architecture. The roofless shell, with its elaborate friezes of religious symbols, stands as a remarkable monument to recusant devotion. The surrounding garden preserves rare Elizabethan earthworks including moated canals, terraces, and an orchard.
shortDescription: An enigmatic unfinished Elizabethan lodge built as a secret symbol of Catholic faith.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Malham Tarn Estate
description: Malham Tarn is one of only eight natural upland lakes in England, set in the heart of the Yorkshire Dales. The surrounding estate encompasses limestone pavement, moorland and flower-rich meadows, and has been a site of scientific research since Charles Darwin visited in 1842.
shortDescription: One of England's few natural upland lakes in the heart of the Yorkshire Dales.

name: Marsden Moor Estate
description: Marsden Moor Estate is a vast expanse of open moorland on the Pennine watershed above the Colne Valley in West Yorkshire. Covering over 5,600 acres of blanket bog, heather moor and rough grassland, it is one of the largest upland estates managed by the National Trust. The moor is important for ground-nesting birds including curlew, golden plover and merlin, and the estate is the site of ongoing peatland restoration work.
shortDescription: 5,600-acre Pennine moorland estate with blanket bog, heather and important ground-nesting bird populations.

name: Melford Hall
description: Melford Hall is a turreted Tudor mansion in the village of Long Melford, home to the Hyde Parker family since 1786. The house has a distinguished history — Elizabeth I was entertained here lavishly in 1578, and Beatrix Potter was a regular visitor who used the hall as inspiration for The Tale of Jemima Puddle-Duck. The interior features a Regency library, Victorian bedrooms and a collection of Chinese porcelain, while the grounds include a charming walled garden and parkland.
shortDescription: Turreted Tudor mansion at Long Melford with Beatrix Potter connections and a fine walled garden.

name: Minnowburn
description: Minnowburn is a popular riverside and woodland walk on the outskirts of south Belfast where the Minnowburn river meets the River Lagan. The area includes the Giant's Ring, a massive 5,000-year-old henge monument with a central dolmen. Mature woodland, wildflower meadows, and the towpath provide a peaceful escape from the city for walkers and cyclists.
shortDescription: A riverside walk on the edge of Belfast with access to the ancient Giant's Ring henge monument.

name: Mompesson House
description: Mompesson House is an elegant Queen Anne townhouse in Salisbury's Cathedral Close, featured as the Dashwood London townhouse in the 1995 film Sense and Sensibility. The house contains a fine collection of 18th-century drinking glasses and period furnishings. A beautiful walled garden provides a tranquil retreat steps from the cathedral.
shortDescription: Elegant Queen Anne townhouse in Salisbury Cathedral Close with a fine glass collection and walled garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Monk's House
description: Monk's House is a modest weatherboarded cottage that was the country retreat of Virginia and Leonard Woolf from 1919 until Leonard's death in 1969. The house contains personal belongings, painted furniture, and the couple's eclectic art collection. The tranquil cottage garden, where Virginia wrote in her lodge, overlooks the Ouse Valley.
shortDescription: Virginia Woolf's country cottage retreat with personal belongings and a tranquil writer's garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Montacute House
description: Montacute House is one of the finest Elizabethan mansions in England, built in the 1590s from local Ham Hill stone with a magnificent Long Gallery — the longest in the country. The house displays an outstanding collection of Tudor and Elizabethan portraits on loan from the National Portrait Gallery. The formal gardens include mixed borders, a raised walkway, and an 18th-century landscape park.
shortDescription: A masterpiece of Elizabethan architecture with England's longest Long Gallery and Tudor portraits.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Morden Hall Park
description: Morden Hall Park is a green oasis in the heart of suburban south London, 125 acres of parkland, wetland and waterways along the River Wandle. Once a deer park and later the estate of the Hatfeild snuff milling family, the park features a beautiful rose garden with over 2,000 roses, ancient hay meadows, a network of waterways and wetlands that support kingfishers and herons, and historic buildings including a former snuff mill.
shortDescription: A 125-acre green oasis in south London with riverside walks, wetlands and a rose garden of 2,000 blooms.

name: Moseley Old Hall
description: Moseley Old Hall is an Elizabethan farmhouse near Wolverhampton where King Charles II hid in a secret hole after his defeat at the Battle of Worcester in 1651, one of the most dramatic episodes of the English Civil War. The house still contains the bed in which the fugitive king slept and the priest hole where he concealed himself from Parliamentarian soldiers. The knot garden has been recreated in 17th-century style with box hedges, gravel paths and period plants that would have been familiar to the king during his desperate flight.
shortDescription: Elizabethan farmhouse where Charles II hid after the Battle of Worcester, with Civil War history and a knot garden.

name: Mottisfont
description: Mottisfont is a country house with medieval priory origins, set in riverside grounds along the Test. It is home to the National Collection of old-fashioned roses, which are at their peak in June. The house features a Rex Whistler drawing room and a contemporary art gallery, while the walled garden and surrounding estate offer year-round interest.
shortDescription: A priory-turned-country house with the National Collection of old roses and Rex Whistler interiors.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Mottistone Gardens
description: Mottistone Gardens is a sheltered hillside garden on the Isle of Wight set around an Elizabethan manor house with medieval origins. The terraced gardens, redesigned in the 20th century, feature Mediterranean-style planting, colourful herbaceous borders and a productive kitchen garden. The surrounding estate offers walks through ancient woodland and up to the Longstone, a Neolithic standing stone on the downs above.
shortDescription: Sheltered hillside garden on the Isle of Wight with Mediterranean planting and an Elizabethan manor.

name: Mount Grace Priory
description: Mount Grace Priory is the best-preserved Carthusian monastery in England, founded in 1398 in the foothills of the North York Moors. The ruins reveal the individual monks' cells with their own gardens and water supply, reflecting the Carthusian order's emphasis on solitary contemplation. One cell has been reconstructed and furnished to show how the monks lived. The site is managed by English Heritage with the surrounding land in National Trust care.
shortDescription: Best-preserved Carthusian monastery in England with reconstructed monks' cells and individual gardens.

name: Mount Stewart
description: Mount Stewart is a magnificent 18th-century house and garden on the shores of Strangford Lough in County Down. The gardens, created by Edith, Lady Londonderry in the 1920s, are among the finest in the National Trust's care, featuring exotic planting thriving in the mild microclimate. The house contains an outstanding collection of art and furnishings accumulated by the Londonderry family.
shortDescription: A magnificent 18th-century house on Strangford Lough with world-renowned gardens by Lady Londonderry.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Mow Cop Castle
description: Mow Cop Castle is a dramatic folly built in 1754 as a summerhouse and designed to look like a ruined medieval castle. Perched at 335 metres on the Cheshire-Staffordshire border, it offers spectacular views across the Cheshire Plain and is the birthplace of Primitive Methodism.
shortDescription: A dramatic 1754 castle folly on the Cheshire-Staffordshire border with panoramic views.

name: Mr Straw's House
description: Mr Straw's House is an Edwardian semi-detached house in Worksop preserved almost exactly as the Straw family left it. The brothers William and Walter Straw changed very little after their parents' deaths, creating a time capsule of early 20th-century domestic life. Visits are by timed ticket only, offering an intimate glimpse into a remarkably ordinary yet fascinating household.
shortDescription: An Edwardian time-capsule house in Worksop, preserved untouched since the early 20th century.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Murlough National Nature Reserve
description: Murlough National Nature Reserve is one of Ireland's first nature reserves, protecting a fragile sand dune system at the foot of the Mourne Mountains. The dunes are among the oldest in Ireland, dating back 6,000 years, and support rare plants, butterflies, and birds. A boardwalk trail leads through the dunes to a sandy beach with views of the Mournes.
shortDescription: One of Ireland's oldest nature reserves with ancient sand dunes at the foot of the Mourne Mountains.

name: Nether Alderley Mill
description: Nether Alderley Mill is a rare 16th-century watermill hidden beneath the sandstone ridge of Alderley Edge. The mill, powered by an overshot waterwheel, still grinds flour on demonstration days using its original stone mechanisms. The atmospheric interior and woodland setting provide a glimpse into centuries of rural Cheshire life.
shortDescription: Rare 16th-century watermill beneath Alderley Edge, still grinding flour on demonstration days.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Newark Park
description: Newark Park is a Tudor hunting lodge dramatically sited on the Cotswold escarpment, later converted into a country house with Georgian and Victorian additions. The house was rescued from near-dereliction in the 1970s by the architect and craftsman Michael Claydon, whose personal collection fills the rooms. The garden and estate offer spectacular views over the Ozleworth Valley towards the Severn Estuary and Wales.
shortDescription: A Tudor hunting lodge perched on the Cotswold escarpment with spectacular valley views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Nostell Priory
description: Nostell Priory is a Palladian mansion near Wakefield, built on the site of a medieval priory and completed by Robert Adam in the 1760s. The house contains one of the finest collections of Chippendale furniture in the world, made specifically for the property. The parkland, landscaped by Stephen Switzer, includes a lake, an adventure playground and lakeside walks.
shortDescription: Palladian mansion near Wakefield with the finest collection of Chippendale furniture in the world.

name: Nuffield Place
description: Nuffield Place was the modest home of William Morris, Viscount Nuffield, the founder of Morris Motors and one of the greatest philanthropists of the 20th century. Despite his enormous wealth, Morris chose to live simply in this comfortable 1930s house filled with his personal belongings, from his collection of clocks to his Wolseley car in the garage. The house and gardens offer a fascinating glimpse into the private life of the man who shaped modern British industry.
shortDescription: Modest 1930s home of Morris Motors founder Viscount Nuffield, filled with personal treasures and period gardens.

name: Nunnington Hall
description: Nunnington Hall is a beautiful manor house in the Ryedale valley of the North York Moors, with parts dating from the Tudor and Stuart periods. The house contains panelled rooms, tapestries, and the Carlisle Collection of miniature rooms displayed in the attic. Organic gardens lead down to the River Rye, and the surrounding countryside offers gentle walks.
shortDescription: Tudor and Stuart manor in Ryedale with miniature rooms, organic gardens and river walks.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Nymans
description: Nymans is one of the great gardens of the Sussex Weald, created over successive generations by the Messel family. The intimate garden rooms are filled with rare plant collections and romantic topiary, while the ruins of the medieval-style house form a dramatic backdrop. The estate also includes woodland and farmland walks.
shortDescription: A celebrated Sussex garden of intimate rooms, rare plants, and romantic house ruins.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Old Harry Rocks
description: Old Harry Rocks are dramatic chalk formations at the eastern end of the Jurassic Coast in Dorset, marking the start of the South West Coast Path. These iconic sea stacks and arches have been sculpted by the sea over millions of years and form part of the Isle of Purbeck.
shortDescription: Iconic chalk sea stacks and arches on the Jurassic Coast in Dorset.

name: Old Soar Manor
description: Old Soar Manor is a rare surviving example of a medieval knight's dwelling, dating from around 1290. The stone-built solar block, with its chapel and undercroft, provides a fascinating glimpse into life in a 13th-century manor house.
shortDescription: A rare surviving medieval knight's dwelling dating from around 1290.

name: Orford Ness
description: Orford Ness is Europe's largest vegetated shingle spit, a wild and hauntingly beautiful landscape stretching along the Suffolk coast. This remote peninsula has a remarkable secret history as a military test site where radar, atomic weapons triggers and other Cold War technologies were developed behind closed doors. Now returned to nature, the Ness is home to rare shingle plants, breeding birds and the eerie remains of the pagoda-shaped test buildings.
shortDescription: Europe's largest shingle spit with a secret Cold War history and rare wildlife on the Suffolk coast.

name: Ormesby Hall
description: Ormesby Hall is a mid-18th-century Palladian mansion on the outskirts of Middlesbrough, a surprising oasis of Georgian elegance surrounded by modern suburbs. The house was built for the Pennyman family and features a fine entrance hall with intricate plasterwork attributed to the Italian stuccodore Cortese, and a striking Victorian laundry and stable block designed by the celebrated architect Detmar Blow. The walled garden has been restored with herbaceous borders and a cutting garden, while the wider estate includes woodland walks and a model railway.
shortDescription: Georgian Palladian mansion near Middlesbrough with fine plasterwork, a Victorian stable block and walled garden.

name: Osterley Park and House
description: Osterley Park is one of the last great country estates in London, a grand Neo-classical villa redesigned by Robert Adam in the 1760s for the Child banking family. The lavish interiors are among Adam's finest, with Etruscan dressing rooms and a state bed. The surrounding parkland includes ancient oak avenues, a lake, and working farm.
shortDescription: A grand Robert Adam Neo-classical villa with lavish interiors set in one of London's last great estates.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Overbeck's
description: Overbeck's is a striking Edwardian house perched above the Salcombe estuary with spectacular views across the South Devon coast. Named after its last owner, the eccentric inventor and scientist Otto Overbeck, the house contains his quirky collection of curiosities. The six-acre garden is one of the most remarkable in the country, where exotic plants from the Mediterranean, South Africa and South America thrive in the mild maritime climate.
shortDescription: Edwardian house above Salcombe with an eccentric inventor's collection and exotic subtropical garden.

name: Owletts
description: Owletts is a red-brick Charles II house in Cobham, Kent, notable for its fine plasterwork ceiling and staircase. The house was the family home of Sir Herbert Baker, the renowned architect who designed the Bank of England and government buildings in New Delhi and Pretoria. The modest garden features a sunken parterre and mature trees.
shortDescription: Charles II red-brick house in Cobham, family home of the architect Sir Herbert Baker.

name: Oxburgh Hall
description: Oxburgh Hall is a stunning moated manor house built by the Bedingfeld family in 1482, who have lived here for over 500 years. The imposing Tudor gatehouse is one of the finest in England, and the house contains needlework by Mary, Queen of Scots. After a major conservation project, the house showcases both its medieval origins and later alterations.
shortDescription: A moated Tudor manor with a magnificent gatehouse and needlework by Mary, Queen of Scots.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Packwood House
description: Packwood House is a beautifully restored Tudor house surrounded by one of the most famous topiary gardens in England. The Yew Garden, said to represent the Sermon on the Mount, features towering yew trees shaped over centuries. The interiors were lovingly furnished in the 1920s-30s by Baron Ash with tapestries, needlework, and fine furniture to create an idealised Tudor home.
shortDescription: A Tudor house with one of England's most renowned topiary gardens representing the Sermon on the Mount.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Parke
description: Parke is a riverside estate on the edge of Bovey Tracey serving as the gateway to Dartmoor National Park. The parkland surrounds a Georgian house set above the River Bovey, with ancient woodland, meadows and a restored walled garden. The estate is home to diverse wildlife including otters, kingfishers and rare greater horseshoe bats.
shortDescription: Dartmoor gateway estate at Bovey Tracey with riverside walks, ancient woodland and a walled garden.

name: Patterson's Spade Mill
description: Patterson's Spade Mill near Templepatrick is the last surviving water-driven spade mill in daily use in the British Isles. Founded in 1919, the mill demonstrates the complete spade-making process from hammer to finish using water-powered trip hammers. Guided tours show visitors the traditional techniques that once served farming communities across Ireland.
shortDescription: The last working water-driven spade mill in the British Isles, demonstrating traditional craftsmanship.

name: Paycocke's House
description: Paycocke's House is a richly ornamented Tudor merchant's house in the centre of Coggeshall, Essex, built around 1509 by John Paycocke, a prosperous wool merchant. The house is famous for its remarkable carved wood panelling and elaborate ceiling beams, considered among the finest examples of domestic Tudor woodwork in England. After centuries of alteration and decline, the house was rescued and restored by Noel Buxton in the early 20th century. The cottage garden behind the house is planted with traditional flowers and features a view of the River Blackwater.
shortDescription: Richly carved Tudor wool merchant's house in Coggeshall with exceptional 16th-century woodwork.

name: Peckover House
description: Peckover House is an elegant Georgian townhouse in the heart of Wisbech, home to the Peckover banking family for over 150 years. The house contains fine plasterwork, wood panelling and an outstanding collection of paintings. The real treasure lies behind the house — a two-acre Victorian garden featuring orangery, summer houses, croquet lawn, herbaceous borders and over 60 varieties of rose.
