shortDescription: Georgian townhouse in Wisbech with fine interiors and a hidden two-acre Victorian garden of roses and borders.

name: Penrhyn Castle
description: Penrhyn Castle is a monumental neo-Norman castle near Bangor, built in the early 19th century for the Pennant family with wealth from Jamaican sugar plantations and Welsh slate quarries. The extravagant interior includes a one-tonne slate bed made for Queen Victoria and an outstanding collection of Old Master paintings. The castle also tells the story of the Great Strike of 1900-03 at the Penrhyn Quarry.
shortDescription: A colossal neo-Norman castle near Bangor built on slate wealth, with a one-tonne slate bed.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Penrose
description: Penrose is a 1,600-acre estate surrounding Loe Pool, the largest natural freshwater lake in Cornwall, separated from the sea by the shingle bar of Loe Bar. The estate encompasses woodland, farmland and coastline, with paths circling the lake through ancient oak woods and past the ornamental grounds of Penrose House. Loe Pool is steeped in Arthurian legend as the lake into which Sir Bedivere cast Excalibur.
shortDescription: Cornish estate surrounding Loe Pool, the county's largest natural lake, steeped in Arthurian legend.

name: Penshaw Monument
description: Penshaw Monument is a striking half-sized replica of the Temple of Hephaestus in Athens, perched dramatically on top of Penshaw Hill in Sunderland. Built in 1844 to commemorate John George Lambton, the first Earl of Durham and the first Governor of Canada, the monument is a much-loved local landmark visible for miles across the surrounding countryside. Guided tours allow visitors to climb the internal staircase for panoramic views across Wearside.
shortDescription: Dramatic Greek-style temple on a hilltop near Sunderland, a beloved North East landmark since 1844.

name: Petworth House
description: Petworth House is a magnificent late 17th-century mansion housing one of the finest art collections in the care of the National Trust, including paintings by Turner, Van Dyck, and Reynolds. The 700-acre deer park, landscaped by Capability Brown, is one of the best surviving examples of his work. The Servants' Quarters offer a fascinating glimpse below stairs.
shortDescription: A grand mansion with an outstanding art collection and a 700-acre Capability Brown deer park.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Pilsdon Pen
description: Pilsdon Pen is an Iron Age hillfort and one of the highest points in Dorset, rising to 277 metres. The exposed summit grassland supports a rich variety of wildflowers and butterflies. From the top, walkers enjoy extensive views over the Marshwood Vale, the Jurassic Coast and into neighbouring Devon and Somerset.
shortDescription: High Iron Age hillfort with wildflower-rich grassland and views to the Jurassic Coast.

name: Plas Newydd
description: Plas Newydd is an elegant 18th-century mansion on the shores of the Menai Strait on Anglesey, home to the Marquesses of Anglesey. The house contains a spectacular 58-foot mural by Rex Whistler, one of the largest paintings on canvas in Britain. The gardens and grounds offer stunning views across the strait to the mountains of Snowdonia, and include a rhododendron garden and Australasian arboretum.
shortDescription: An elegant Anglesey mansion with Rex Whistler's largest mural and Menai Strait views to Snowdonia.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Plas yn Rhiw
description: Plas yn Rhiw is a small manor house with an ornamental garden and stunning views over Cardigan Bay on the Llyn Peninsula. The house dates partly from the medieval period and was rescued from neglect by the Keating sisters in 1938. Its sheltered gardens feature box hedges, grass paths, and flowering trees set against a backdrop of Snowdonia.
shortDescription: A rescued medieval manor house with ornamental gardens overlooking Cardigan Bay on the Llyn Peninsula.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Polesden Lacey
description: Polesden Lacey is an Edwardian country house set in 1,400 acres of rolling Surrey Hills countryside. The lavish interiors reflect the glamorous Edwardian hostess Mrs Ronald Greville, who entertained royalty and high society. The walled rose garden, long walk, and views over the North Downs make it a popular year-round destination.
shortDescription: An Edwardian mansion with lavish interiors, a famous rose garden, and sweeping Surrey Hills views.

name: Portstewart Strand
description: Portstewart Strand is a magnificent two-mile stretch of golden sandy beach backed by dunes on the north coast near Portstewart. The dune system is an important nature reserve supporting over 450 plant species including several rare orchids. The beach is popular for swimming and surfing, and vehicles can drive directly onto the sand during summer months.
shortDescription: A magnificent two-mile golden beach backed by species-rich dunes on the north coast.

name: Powis Castle and Garden
description: Powis Castle is a medieval fortress that has been continuously inhabited for over 700 years, rising above world-famous Italianate terraced gardens. The castle houses one of the finest collections of paintings, sculpture, furniture and textiles in Wales, alongside the Clive Museum of treasures from India. The terraces, originally laid out in the late 17th century, are among the finest in Britain.
shortDescription: A 700-year-old medieval castle above world-famous Italianate terraced gardens in Powys.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Prior Park Landscape Garden
description: Prior Park Landscape Garden is an intimate 18th-century landscape garden created by the entrepreneur Ralph Allen with advice from Alexander Pope and Capability Brown. The centrepiece is one of only four Palladian bridges in the world, framing views across the sweeping valley to the city of Bath. The garden features a serpentine lake, wilderness walk, and a restored 18th-century rock gate and cascade.
shortDescription: An 18th-century landscape garden with a rare Palladian bridge and sweeping views of Bath.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Quarry Bank
description: Quarry Bank is one of Britain's greatest industrial heritage sites, centred on a Georgian cotton mill in the wooded Bollin Valley at Styal. Founded by Samuel Greg in 1784, the mill retains working textile machinery powered by Europe's most powerful waterwheel. The Apprentice House reveals the harsh lives of child workers, while the surrounding estate includes gardens and riverside walks.
shortDescription: Georgian cotton mill at Styal with working machinery, Apprentice House and riverside walks.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Quebec House
description: Quebec House is a red-brick Tudor house in the centre of Westerham, Kent, the childhood home of General James Wolfe, who famously captured Quebec from the French in 1759 and established British rule in Canada. The house contains family portraits, prints and memorabilia relating to the Battle of Quebec, along with period furnishings that evoke 18th-century domestic life. The garden includes an exhibition about the battle and the wider Seven Years' War.
shortDescription: Tudor childhood home of General Wolfe in Westerham, with memorabilia from the capture of Quebec in 1759.

name: Rainham Hall
description: Rainham Hall is an elegant Georgian townhouse built in 1729 for Captain John Harle, a merchant and wharf owner in the Thames-side village of Rainham. The red-brick Queen Anne-style house retains many original features including fine panelling, a carved staircase and period fireplaces. The walled garden has been restored with planting inspired by the 18th-century landscape.
shortDescription: Elegant 1729 Georgian townhouse in east London with original panelling and a restored walled garden.

name: Red House
description: Red House was designed by Philip Webb and William Morris in 1859 as Morris's family home and is regarded as the birthplace of the Arts and Crafts movement. The house features original Morris wall paintings, stained glass by Edward Burne-Jones, and innovative Gothic Revival architecture. The intimate garden was designed by Morris as a series of outdoor rooms.
shortDescription: William Morris's pioneering Arts and Crafts home with original wall paintings and Burne-Jones glass.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Rhossili & South Gower Coast
description: Rhossili Bay is regularly voted one of the best beaches in the UK and Europe, a three-mile sweep of golden sand on the tip of the Gower Peninsula. The dramatic coastline includes the tidal island of Worm's Head, spectacular cliffs and the remains of a medieval village.
shortDescription: Award-winning three-mile beach with dramatic cliffs and tidal island on the Gower Peninsula.

name: Rievaulx Terrace
description: Rievaulx Terrace is an 18th-century landscaped terrace above the ruins of Rievaulx Abbey in the Rye Valley. The half-mile grass terrace was designed to provide a series of carefully framed views down to the medieval abbey below. Two classical temples punctuate the walk, one containing a painted ceiling and period furnishings, offering a perfect marriage of art and landscape.
shortDescription: Georgian landscaped terrace with classical temples and framed views of Rievaulx Abbey ruins.

name: Ringstead Bay
description: Ringstead Bay is a quiet shingle and sand beach on the Jurassic Coast between Weymouth and Lulworth. The sheltered bay is backed by wildflower-rich cliffs and offers views across to the Isle of Portland. It is a popular spot for swimming, fossil hunting and coastal walks along the South West Coast Path.
shortDescription: Sheltered Jurassic Coast bay with shingle beach, wildflower cliffs and fossil hunting.

name: Robin Hood's Bay
description: Robin Hood's Bay is a stretch of dramatic coastline on the North Yorkshire coast south of Whitby, encompassing rocky shore platforms rich in fossils and Jurassic geology. The National Trust protects the surrounding clifftop farmland and coastal footpaths, which form part of the Cleveland Way. The area is renowned for its rock pools, fossil hunting and spectacular views along the Heritage Coast.
shortDescription: Fossil-rich North Yorkshire coastline near Whitby with dramatic cliffs and Jurassic rock pools.

name: Roe Estuary
description: The Roe Estuary is a tranquil stretch of mudflats and salt marsh where the River Roe meets Lough Foyle in County Londonderry. It is an important wintering ground for migratory birds including whooper swans, pale-bellied brent geese, and bar-tailed godwits. The reserve offers birdwatching hides and walking paths along the estuary.
shortDescription: A tranquil estuary on Lough Foyle that is an important wintering ground for migratory birds.

name: Roseberry Topping
description: Roseberry Topping is a distinctive half-cone-shaped hill on the northern edge of the North York Moors, often called the Matterhorn of Cleveland. Rising to 320 metres, the summit collapsed on one side due to mining and geological faulting, creating its dramatic profile. The steep but popular walk to the top rewards with panoramic views from the Pennines to the coast.
shortDescription: Distinctive half-cone hill on the North York Moors edge with panoramic summit views, known as Cleveland's Matterhorn.

name: Rowallane Garden
description: Rowallane Garden is a 52-acre garden near Saintfield in County Down, renowned for its spectacular collection of rhododendrons, azaleas, and rare bulbs set within a walled garden and natural rock garden. Created over generations by the Rev. John Moore and his nephew Hugh Armytage Moore, the garden has an informal style that complements its drumlin landscape. Spring and autumn are particularly stunning.
shortDescription: A 52-acre garden near Saintfield renowned for rare rhododendrons and a natural rock garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Rufford Old Hall
description: Rufford Old Hall is one of Lancashire's finest Tudor buildings, famous for its extravagant Great Hall with an intricately carved movable wooden screen. A young William Shakespeare is said to have performed here in the 1580s. The Victorian wing houses the Hesketh family's eclectic collections, and the gardens include a topiary avenue and seasonal herbaceous borders.
shortDescription: Fine Tudor hall in Lancashire where Shakespeare may have performed, with a famous carved screen.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Runnymede
description: Runnymede is the historic meadow on the banks of the River Thames where King John sealed the Magna Carta in 1215, establishing the principles of justice and liberty that have influenced democracies around the world. The riverside landscape includes the Magna Carta Memorial built by the American Bar Association, the John F. Kennedy Memorial set within an acre of English ground given to the United States, and the Commonwealth Air Forces Memorial commemorating over 20,000 airmen who lost their lives in World War II.
shortDescription: Historic Thames-side meadow where the Magna Carta was sealed in 1215, with three poignant memorials.

name: Saltram
description: Saltram is a magnificent George II mansion set in a landscaped parkland on the outskirts of Plymouth. The house contains outstanding Robert Adam interiors, original Chinese wallpapers and an important collection of Joshua Reynolds paintings. The grounds include a beautiful orangery, formal gardens and 500 acres of parkland with views across the Plym estuary.
shortDescription: Grand Georgian mansion near Plymouth with Robert Adam interiors and sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Sandham Memorial Chapel
description: Sandham Memorial Chapel houses one of the greatest sets of paintings created in Britain in the 20th century — a cycle of monumental murals by Stanley Spencer inspired by his experiences as a soldier in World War I. The paintings, which took Spencer six years to complete, cover every wall of the purpose-built chapel and culminate in a vast Resurrection scene on the altar wall. Built in the 1920s to commemorate Lieutenant Henry Willoughby Sandham, the chapel is often compared to Giotto's Arena Chapel in Padua.
shortDescription: Purpose-built chapel housing Stanley Spencer's monumental World War I murals, a masterpiece of 20th-century art.

name: Sandscale Haws National Nature Reserve
description: Sandscale Haws is a National Nature Reserve on the Duddon Estuary, comprising a large dune system rich in wildlife. The reserve supports rare natterjack toads, orchids and over 30 species of butterfly. Boardwalks and paths wind through the dunes, offering views across the estuary to the Lakeland fells and out to the Irish Sea.
shortDescription: A wildlife-rich dune reserve on the Duddon Estuary, home to rare natterjack toads and orchids.

name: Sandymouth Beach
description: Sandymouth is a dramatic stretch of coastline near Bude in North Cornwall, featuring a wide sandy beach backed by twisted cliffs and waterfalls. The unusual rock formations reveal layers of geological history, and the beach is a popular spot for surfing and rockpooling.
shortDescription: A dramatic sandy beach with twisted cliffs and waterfalls near Bude in North Cornwall.

name: Scotney Castle
description: Scotney Castle features a romantic medieval moated castle ruin set in a beautiful wooded valley garden. The picturesque Old Castle, dating from the 14th century, was deliberately retained as a garden feature when the new Victorian country house was built above. The estate includes woodland walks and hop gardens.
shortDescription: A romantic moated castle ruin in a wooded valley garden with a Victorian country house above.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Seaton Delaval Hall
description: Seaton Delaval Hall is considered one of Sir John Vanbrugh's finest architectural works, a theatrical English Baroque masterpiece on the Northumberland coast. The central block was gutted by fire in 1822 and stands as a dramatic shell, while the flanking wings have been carefully restored. The gardens include a parterre, wildflower meadows and a recently restored play garden.
shortDescription: Vanbrugh's theatrical Baroque masterpiece on the Northumberland coast, dramatically fire-scarred and restored.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Shalford Mill
description: Shalford Mill is a picturesque 18th-century watermill on the Tillingbourne stream near Guildford, believed to have been the inspiration for the mill in John Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress. The timber-framed and tile-hung mill was donated to the National Trust in 1932 by an anonymous group calling themselves 'Ferguson's Gang', eccentric masked benefactors who championed the preservation of English rural heritage. The mill retains its original machinery including the wooden waterwheel, grinding stones and grain hopper, offering a glimpse of the rural milling tradition that once sustained communities across the English countryside.
shortDescription: Picturesque 18th-century Surrey watermill, possibly Bunyan's inspiration, saved by the masked Ferguson's Gang.

name: Shaw's Corner
description: Shaw's Corner is the former home of George Bernard Shaw, the Nobel Prize-winning playwright, who lived here from 1906 until his death in 1950. The Edwardian Arts and Crafts house remains much as Shaw left it, with his desk, typewriter and personal belongings offering an intimate portrait of one of the 20th century's greatest literary figures. At the bottom of the garden stands his famous revolving writing hut, designed so he could always work facing the sun.
shortDescription: Edwardian home of Nobel laureate George Bernard Shaw, with his famous revolving garden writing hut.

name: Sheffield Park and Garden
description: Sheffield Park is a stunning 120-acre landscape garden laid out in the 18th century by Capability Brown and later transformed with exotic plantings. Four lakes form the centrepiece, surrounded by rare trees and shrubs that produce breathtaking autumn colour displays. The garden is renowned as one of the finest in England for autumn visits.
shortDescription: A magnificent 120-acre landscape garden with four lakes, famed for its spectacular autumn colours.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Sheringham Park
description: Sheringham Park is a landscape park designed by Humphry Repton, who considered it his finest work. The park is famous for its spectacular display of rhododendrons and azaleas in late spring, with viewing towers offering panoramic views to the North Norfolk coast. The estate includes coastal walks, woodland trails, and a working farm.
shortDescription: Humphry Repton's self-proclaimed finest landscape park, famous for spectacular rhododendron displays.

name: Shugborough Estate
description: Shugborough Estate is the ancestral seat of the Earls of Lichfield, set in 900 acres of Staffordshire parkland. The mansion house contains fine collections of ceramics, silver and paintings, while the surrounding grounds feature neo-classical monuments and a working rare breeds farm. The estate also includes a restored Georgian servants' quarters and a walled garden.
shortDescription: A grand 900-acre Staffordshire estate with a mansion, neo-classical monuments, and working farm.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Shute Barton
description: Shute Barton is one of the most important surviving medieval manor houses in Devon, with parts dating from the late 14th century. The battlemented gatehouse and great hall reflect its origins as a fortified residence of the Bonville family during the Wars of the Roses. The house retains remarkable original features including a massive fireplace and Tudor windows.
shortDescription: Important medieval manor house in Devon with a battlemented gatehouse and 14th-century great hall.

name: Sissinghurst Castle Garden
description: Sissinghurst Castle Garden is one of England's most celebrated gardens, created in the 1930s by Vita Sackville-West and Harold Nicolson. The garden is arranged as a series of intimate 'rooms' divided by hedges and walls, including the famous White Garden. The Elizabethan tower offers panoramic views over the Weald.
shortDescription: Vita Sackville-West's iconic garden rooms, including the famous White Garden, set around an Elizabethan tower.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Sizergh
description: Sizergh has been home to the Strickland family for over 760 years, making it one of the longest-held family homes in England. The medieval castle features a remarkable Elizabethan carved overmantel and impressive panelling. The surrounding estate includes a limestone rock garden, wildflower meadows, an orchard and a lake with stunning views towards the Lakeland fells.
shortDescription: A castle held by the Strickland family for 760 years, with Elizabethan interiors and a rock garden.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Skenfrith Castle
description: Skenfrith Castle is one of the Three Castles of Gwent, built in the early 13th century to guard the route from England into Wales. The castle features an impressive round keep and curtain walls set in a picturesque location beside the River Monnow.
shortDescription: A 13th-century castle of the Three Castles of Gwent beside the River Monnow.

name: Smallhythe Place
description: Smallhythe Place is a charming early 16th-century half-timbered house near Tenterden that was the home of the great Victorian actress Ellen Terry from 1899 until her death in 1928. The house is preserved as a shrine to her career, containing her theatrical costumes, personal possessions and a remarkable collection of memorabilia from the golden age of the Victorian and Edwardian stage. The cottage garden and orchard surround a small barn theatre where performances are still held each summer.
shortDescription: Ellen Terry's half-timbered home near Tenterden, filled with Victorian theatrical costumes and memorabilia.

name: Snowshill Manor
description: Snowshill Manor is a Cotswold manor house containing an extraordinary collection of over 22,000 objects amassed by the eccentric Charles Paget Wade between 1919 and 1956. The collection ranges from Samurai armour and musical instruments to toys and bicycles, all arranged with theatrical flair. The Arts and Crafts-influenced garden is arranged as a series of outdoor rooms with pools, ponds and colourful borders, offering views across the Cotswold countryside.
shortDescription: Cotswold manor housing an eccentric collection of 22,000+ objects from toys to Samurai armour, with Arts and Crafts gardens.

name: Souter Lighthouse
description: Souter Lighthouse was the first lighthouse in the world to be purpose-built for electric light when it opened in 1871. Perched on the clifftops at Marsden between the Tyne and the Wear, it offers panoramic views along the dramatic coastline. The surrounding grasslands are home to wildflowers and nesting seabirds, and the Leas below provide excellent rock-pool exploring.
shortDescription: The world's first purpose-built electric lighthouse, set on dramatic Marsden clifftops.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: South Foreland Lighthouse
description: South Foreland Lighthouse is a Victorian lighthouse on the White Cliffs of Dover, notable as the first lighthouse to use an electric light in 1858 and the site of Marconi's first international radio transmission in 1899. The lighthouse offers panoramic views across the English Channel to France on clear days. Reached by a clifftop walk from the White Cliffs visitor centre, it stands as a monument to British maritime innovation.
shortDescription: Victorian lighthouse on the White Cliffs of Dover, pioneering site for electric light and Marconi's radio.

name: Speke Hall
description: Speke Hall is one of England's finest surviving Tudor timber-framed houses, set in tranquil gardens and woodland on the banks of the Mersey near Liverpool Airport. Built over a century from 1530, the house features priest holes, elaborate plasterwork, and a great hall. The restored Victorian gardens include the Adam and Eve topiaries and a kitchen garden.
shortDescription: Magnificent Tudor timber-framed house on the Mersey with priest holes and Victorian gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Springhill
description: Springhill is a charming 17th-century plantation house near Moneymore in County Londonderry, home to the Lenox-Conyngham family for ten generations. The whitewashed house contains a fine costume collection and a library of rare books. The grounds include a walled garden, beech-lined avenues, and the estate's original outbuildings.
shortDescription: A charming 17th-century plantation house near Moneymore with a fine costume collection and walled garden.

name: Spyway
description: Spyway is a dramatic Dorset countryside property set on the Purbeck ridge above the village of Langton Matravers. The land includes ancient strip lynchets, stone quarries and rich limestone grassland. Walkers can enjoy stunning views over the coast to Dancing Ledge and beyond to the Isle of Wight.
shortDescription: Purbeck ridge countryside with ancient strip lynchets and dramatic coastal views to Dancing Ledge.

name: St Anthony Head
description: St Anthony Head is a scenic headland at the eastern entrance to the Carrick Roads estuary on the Roseland Peninsula in Cornwall. The headland is home to a 19th-century lighthouse and the remains of a World War II gun battery, with coastal paths offering views across Falmouth Bay and towards the Lizard. The sheltered coves and subtropical vegetation make it one of Cornwall's most tranquil coastal walks.
shortDescription: Scenic Roseland Peninsula headland with a 19th-century lighthouse and wartime gun battery remains.

name: St Michael's Mount
description: St Michael's Mount is a tidal island just off the coast of Marazion in Cornwall, crowned by a medieval castle and church. Connected to the mainland by a granite causeway passable at low tide, the island has been a place of pilgrimage, a fortress and a private home for centuries. The castle rooms tell stories from its rich history, while the subtropical gardens cascade down the rocky slopes with exotic plants thriving in the mild maritime climate.
shortDescription: Tidal island off Marazion with a medieval castle, subtropical gardens and a causeway passable at low tide.

name: Stackpole
description: Stackpole is a spectacular coastal estate in south Pembrokeshire encompassing limestone cliffs, lily ponds, woodlands, and the famous Barafundle Bay beach. The estate's eight-mile trail network passes through diverse habitats home to otters, choughs, and rare orchids. Bosherston Lily Ponds are particularly stunning when the water lilies bloom in June.
shortDescription: A stunning Pembrokeshire coastal estate with Barafundle Bay, lily ponds, and limestone cliff walks.

name: Stagshaw Garden and Ambleside
description: Stagshaw Garden is an informal woodland garden on the hillside above Ambleside, created by the former regional National Trust agent Cubby Acland. The garden is celebrated for its magnificent collection of azaleas, rhododendrons and camellias, which create a spectacular display in spring. Paths wind through the garden offering glimpses of Windermere through the trees.
shortDescription: A hillside woodland garden above Ambleside famed for its spring azalea and rhododendron displays.

name: Stainsby Mill
description: Stainsby Mill is a fully working water-powered flour mill on the Hardwick Estate in Derbyshire. Dating from the late 18th century, the mill still grinds flour using traditional methods and provides a fascinating insight into rural milling heritage.
shortDescription: A working water-powered flour mill on the Hardwick Estate in Derbyshire.

name: Standen House and Garden
description: Standen is an outstanding Arts and Crafts house designed by Philip Webb and furnished with William Morris textiles and wallpapers. Built in the 1890s, it is one of the finest examples of the Arts and Crafts movement in Britain. The hillside garden offers beautiful views over Ashdown Forest and includes a quarry garden and conservatory.
shortDescription: A superb Arts and Crafts house with William Morris interiors and hillside Ashdown Forest views.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Staunton Harold Church
description: Staunton Harold Church is one of the few churches built during the Commonwealth period, completed in 1653 by Sir Robert Shirley in defiance of Oliver Cromwell. The church retains its original 17th-century interior virtually unchanged, including box pews, a painted ceiling and a wrought-iron chancel screen. An inscription above the door boldly records that Shirley built it 'when all things sacred were throughout the nation either demolished or profaned'.
shortDescription: Rare Commonwealth-era church built in 1653 in defiance of Cromwell, with its original interior intact.

name: Stoneacre
description: Stoneacre is a half-timbered yeoman's house in the village of Otham near Maidstone, dating from the late 15th century. The house was sympathetically restored in the 1920s by Aymer Vallance, who incorporated architectural salvage from other historic buildings to create an authentic medieval interior. The cottage garden is planted in traditional style with herbaceous borders, roses and fruit trees.
shortDescription: Late 15th-century half-timbered yeoman's house in Kent with a charming cottage garden.

name: Stonehenge Landscape
description: The National Trust cares for 827 hectares of chalk downland surrounding Stonehenge, the most famous prehistoric monument in Europe. The landscape contains hundreds of archaeological features including burial mounds, cursus monuments and the Avenue processional route. While English Heritage manages the stone circle itself, the Trust's surrounding landscape allows visitors to walk freely across the ancient ceremonial terrain.
shortDescription: Chalk downland surrounding Stonehenge with hundreds of prehistoric monuments in the wider ceremonial landscape.

name: Stoneywell
description: Stoneywell is a remarkable Arts and Crafts cottage in Charnwood Forest, designed by the Leicester architect Ernest Gimson in 1899 for his brother Sydney. Built of local stone and slate, the cottage appears to grow organically from the rocky outcrop on which it sits, with rooms at different levels following the contours of the ground. The cottage remained in the Gimson family for over a century, its interior virtually unchanged, preserving an authentic example of Arts and Crafts domestic life. The surrounding woodland garden is rich with bluebells in spring.
shortDescription: Arts and Crafts cottage in Charnwood Forest by Ernest Gimson, growing from the rocks in century-old woodland.

name: Stourhead
description: Stourhead is a grand Palladian mansion set amid one of the finest landscape gardens in the world. Created in the 18th century by Henry Hoare II, the garden features a magnificent lake, classical temples, and rare tree collections. The house contains Chippendale furniture and an outstanding library.
shortDescription: World-famous 18th-century landscape garden with a Palladian mansion, classical temples, and a magnificent lake.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Stowe
description: Stowe is one of the most significant landscape gardens in Europe, with over 40 temples and monuments set across 250 acres of sweeping lawns, lakes, and valleys. Designed by the greatest landscape architects of the 18th century including Bridgeman, Kent, and Capability Brown, it is a masterpiece of the English Landscape movement. The gardens inspired countless imitations worldwide.
shortDescription: One of Europe's finest landscape gardens with over 40 temples across 250 acres of sweeping parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Strangford Lough
description: Strangford Lough is a large sea lough in County Down and one of the most important wildlife habitats in Europe, designated as a Marine Nature Reserve. The lough is home to vast populations of wading birds and wintering wildfowl, as well as seals and rare marine life. The National Trust manages several key access points around the lough's shores and islands.
shortDescription: A vast sea lough in County Down that is one of Europe's most important marine wildlife habitats.

name: Studland Bay
description: Studland Bay is a four-mile sweep of golden sandy beach on the Dorset coast, stretching from Shell Bay to Old Harry Rocks. The beach is backed by a nationally important nature reserve of heathland, sand dunes and freshwater bog, home to all six native reptile species. The area offers excellent swimming, kayaking and walking with views across Poole Harbour.
shortDescription: Four miles of golden sand backed by rare heathland, with views to Old Harry Rocks.

name: Sudbury Hall
description: Sudbury Hall is a 17th-century country house renowned for its lavish interiors, including intricate plasterwork ceilings, elaborate wood carvings by Grinling Gibbons, and a magnificent Great Staircase. The house also hosts the National Trust Museum of Childhood, exploring what it was like to grow up in different periods of history. The grounds feature a lakeside walk and walled garden.
shortDescription: A 17th-century house with stunning decorative interiors and the National Trust Museum of Childhood.

name: Sunnycroft
description: Sunnycroft is a rare surviving example of a suburban Victorian villa in Wellington, Shropshire, the kind of genteel family home that once lined the streets of every English market town but has largely been swept away by development. The house and its contents were left virtually unchanged by the last owner, Joan Lander, who bequeathed it to the National Trust in 1997. The interiors are filled with original furniture, family photographs and everyday objects, creating an extraordinarily evocative time capsule of middle-class domestic life from the Edwardian era to the 1990s.
shortDescription: Rare Victorian suburban villa in Wellington, a time capsule of domestic life from the 1880s to the 1990s.

name: Sutton Hoo
description: Sutton Hoo is one of Britain's most important archaeological sites, famous for the 1939 discovery of an Anglo-Saxon ship burial containing a wealth of treasures including a gold helmet, sword and jewellery. The exhibition hall tells the story of this remarkable find and the people it belonged to, while the burial mounds overlook the River Deben estuary. The estate also includes Tranmer House, the home of Edith Pretty who commissioned the original excavation.
shortDescription: Anglo-Saxon royal burial site made famous by the 1939 ship burial discovery, with an exhibition hall and estate walks.

name: Sutton House
description: Sutton House is a rare Tudor red-brick house in Hackney, the oldest domestic building in East London, built in 1535 for Sir Ralph Sadleir, a courtier of Henry VIII. Behind its Georgian facade lie original Tudor rooms including linenfold panelling, carved fireplaces and a painted Jacobean staircase. The Breaker's Yard, added later, offers a contrast with its contemporary art exhibitions and community spaces. The house has been everything from a girls' school to a squat during its varied history.
shortDescription: East London's oldest domestic building — a Tudor red-brick house in Hackney with 500 years of stories.

name: Tarn Hows and Coniston
description: Tarn Hows is one of the most visited and photographed spots in the Lake District, a picturesque man-made tarn surrounded by woodland and hills with views to the Langdale Pikes and the Old Man of Coniston. An easy circular path of about a mile and a half loops around the tarn, making it accessible for families and those with limited mobility. The site was donated to the Trust by Beatrix Potter.
shortDescription: One of the Lake District's most iconic viewpoints, a picturesque tarn donated by Beatrix Potter.

name: Tattershall Castle
description: Tattershall Castle is a dramatic red-brick tower keep built in the 1430s by Ralph Cromwell, Lord Treasurer of England. Rising six storeys and over 30 metres high, it is one of the finest examples of medieval brick building in England. The castle was rescued from demolition by Lord Curzon in 1911, who donated it to the National Trust along with its impressive Gothic fireplaces.
shortDescription: A towering medieval red-brick castle keep built for a Lord Treasurer of England.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Tatton Park
description: Tatton Park is a grand neo-classical mansion set in 1,000 acres of deer park near Knutsford. The opulent Egerton family home contains Gillow furniture, a picture gallery and servants' quarters. The gardens are among the most important in England, featuring a Japanese garden, Italian garden, glasshouses and the national collection of tree ferns.
shortDescription: Neo-classical mansion near Knutsford with opulent interiors and nationally important gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Templetown Mausoleum
description: Templetown Mausoleum is an 18th-century mausoleum designed by Robert Adam, standing in the grounds of Castle Upton near Templepatrick. Built around 1789 as a memorial to the Hon. Arthur Upton, it is considered one of Adam's finest works in Ireland. The neoclassical structure features elegant proportions and decorative urns.
shortDescription: An elegant 18th-century neoclassical mausoleum designed by Robert Adam near Templepatrick.

name: The Argory
description: The Argory is a handsome 1820s Irish gentry house on a wooded hill above the River Blackwater in County Armagh. The house is lit by an original acetylene gas plant, one of very few surviving in the British Isles, and the interiors remain largely as the MacGeough-Bond family left them. The grounds include a riverside walk, pleasure garden, and rose garden.
shortDescription: An 1820s gentry house above the River Blackwater with original acetylene gas lighting and pleasure gardens.

name: The Beatles' Childhood Homes
description: The Beatles' Childhood Homes are two houses in Liverpool where John Lennon and Paul McCartney grew up. Mendips, John's childhood home with Aunt Mimi, is a 1930s semi-detached where he wrote early songs. 20 Forthlin Road, Paul's terraced council house, is where many of the earliest Lennon-McCartney songs were composed. Both can only be visited on guided minibus tours from the city centre.
shortDescription: John Lennon's and Paul McCartney's childhood homes, accessible by guided minibus tour only.

name: The Courts Garden
description: The Courts Garden in the village of Holt is an English country garden of great charm and variety, designed around a mid-18th-century house originally used as the village's court and cloth-weaving centre. The garden blends formal and naturalistic styles with herbaceous borders, topiary, a lily pond, an arboretum and a kitchen garden. Unusual plants thrive in sheltered corners, and the garden is particularly striking in summer when the borders reach their peak. The garden was developed from the 1920s by Major T.C.E. Goff and later Lady Cecilie Goff.
shortDescription: Charming English country garden at Holt with herbaceous borders, topiary and a lily pond beside a Georgian house.

name: The Crown Bar
description: The Crown Bar is a magnificently preserved Victorian gin palace on Great Victoria Street in Belfast, and the most famous pub in Northern Ireland. Its ornate interior features carved woodwork, coloured glass, decorative tiles, and original gas lighting. The National Trust acquired the building in 1978 to protect this outstanding example of Victorian craftsmanship.
shortDescription: Belfast's most famous Victorian gin palace with ornate carved woodwork and original gas lighting.

name: The Firs (Elgar's Birthplace)
description: The Firs is the modest cottage in Lower Broadheath where the composer Sir Edward Elgar was born in 1857. The house and adjacent museum contain a rich collection of Elgar's manuscripts, personal belongings and concert programmes, tracing his journey from a Worcestershire music shop owner's son to England's greatest composer. The garden has been restored to reflect how it would have appeared during Elgar's childhood.
shortDescription: Birthplace cottage of composer Sir Edward Elgar with manuscripts, personal belongings and a restored garden.

name: The Fleece Inn
description: The Fleece Inn is a half-timbered medieval farmhouse in Bretforton, Worcestershire, that has served as a pub since 1848. The building dates from the 15th century and was bequeathed to the National Trust in 1977 by Lola Taplin, whose family had owned it for over 500 years. The interior retains its original pewter collection, flagstone floors and inglenook fireplace, and the pub hosts the annual asparagus auction each May.
shortDescription: Medieval half-timbered pub in Bretforton owned by one family for over 500 years, now a working NT inn.

name: The Greyfriars
description: The Greyfriars is a timber-framed merchant's house built in 1480 on Friar Street in Worcester, one of the finest examples of a late medieval town house in England. The house was rescued from demolition in 1946 by the Matley Moore family, who restored it and filled the panelled rooms with period furniture and textiles. The walled garden behind the house provides a peaceful retreat from the city centre with herbaceous borders and an orchard.
shortDescription: Fine 15th-century timber-framed merchant's house in Worcester with panelled rooms and a walled garden.

name: The Langdales
description: The Langdales encompass two of the Lake District's most dramatic valleys, Great Langdale and Little Langdale, surrounded by some of the finest peaks in England. The distinctive profile of the Langdale Pikes is one of the most recognisable mountain silhouettes in Britain. The valleys contain a Neolithic stone axe factory site and offer walks ranging from gentle riverside paths to challenging fell scrambles.
shortDescription: Twin Lakeland valleys beneath the iconic Langdale Pikes, with walks from gentle to challenging.

name: The Needles Old Battery
description: The Needles Old Battery is a Victorian coastal fort built in 1862 at the western tip of the Isle of Wight to defend against the threat of French invasion. Perched high on the chalk headland above the famous Needles rocks, the fort includes original gun barrels, a powder magazine and a 60-foot tunnel leading to a searchlight emplacement with breathtaking views. The site was later used as a Cold War rocket testing facility in the 1950s.
shortDescription: Victorian cliff-top fort above the Needles with gun emplacements, a tunnel and Cold War rocket history.

name: The Vyne
description: The Vyne is a Tudor mansion built in the early 16th century for Lord Sandys, Lord Chamberlain to Henry VIII. It was the first country house in England to have a classical portico, added in the 1650s. The house contains fine Tudor chapel stained glass, a Palladian staircase, and the atmospheric Stone Gallery.
shortDescription: A Tudor mansion with England's first classical portico, fine stained glass, and a Palladian staircase.

name: The Weir Garden
description: The Weir Garden is a riverside garden perched on the banks of the River Wye near Hereford, offering spectacular views across to the Welsh hills and Black Mountains. The garden cascades down a steep slope to the river through a naturalistic planting of bulbs, wildflowers and specimen trees. It is particularly celebrated for its spring display of snowdrops, crocuses and daffodils.
shortDescription: Riverside garden above the Wye with views to the Black Mountains, celebrated for spring bulbs.

name: The Workhouse, Southwell
description: The Workhouse in Southwell is the best-preserved workhouse in England, built in 1824 to the design of the Reverend John T. Becher as a model for the national system established by the Poor Law Amendment Act of 1834. The building's austere rooms, exercise yards and segregated quarters for men, women and children tell the harsh story of poverty and welfare in 19th-century England. The adjacent infirmary, restored by the National Trust, reveals how the workhouse also served as a rudimentary hospital. The Workhouse offers a powerful and sobering insight into social history that shaped the modern welfare state.
shortDescription: Best-preserved workhouse in England, telling the story of 19th-century poverty and the origins of welfare.

name: Tintagel Old Post Office
description: Tintagel Old Post Office is a remarkably well-preserved 14th-century stone longhouse in the heart of Tintagel village. One of the oldest domestic buildings in Cornwall, its dramatically undulating slate roof has become an iconic image of the village. The building served as a receiving office for mail in the Victorian era and has been furnished to recreate that period, with one room set up as a parlour and another as the postal office complete with original letter rack and stamps.
shortDescription: 14th-century stone longhouse in Tintagel with a famously wavy roof, once a Victorian post office.

name: Tintinhull Garden
description: Tintinhull Garden is an exquisite small garden created around a 17th-century farmhouse in Somerset, widely considered one of the finest examples of a 20th-century English garden. The garden is designed as a series of interconnected outdoor rooms, each with its own colour theme and character, linked by paths and vistas. Planted by Phyllis Reiss between the 1930s and 1960s, and later maintained by the celebrated garden writer Penelope Hobhouse, the garden demonstrates the art of garden design on an intimate scale.
shortDescription: Exquisite small garden of interconnected rooms around a 17th-century Somerset farmhouse, a masterclass in planting design.

name: Townend
description: Townend is a well-preserved 17th-century Lakeland farmhouse in the village of Troutbeck, home to the Browne family for over 300 years. The house contains an exceptional collection of carved woodwork, books, papers and domestic implements that record the life of a prosperous yeoman farming family. It is one of the finest examples of Lake District vernacular architecture.
shortDescription: A 17th-century Troutbeck farmhouse with exceptional carved woodwork, home to one family for 300 years.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Toys Hill
description: Toys Hill is the highest point in Kent, a wooded hilltop in the Greensand Ridge offering far-reaching views across the Weald. The ancient woodland of beech and oak was devastated by the Great Storm of 1987 but has regenerated naturally, creating a mosaic of habitats for wildlife. The site was a favourite retreat of Octavia Hill, co-founder of the National Trust, who lived nearby at Crockham Hill.
shortDescription: Kent's highest point with regenerated ancient woodland, a favourite retreat of NT co-founder Octavia Hill.

name: Treasurer's House
description: Treasurer's House in York stands in the shadow of York Minster on a site occupied since Roman times. Named after the medieval Treasurers of York Minster who lived here, the present house dates mainly from the 17th and 18th centuries but incorporates medieval fabric. It was lavishly restored by the industrialist Frank Green between 1897 and 1930 and filled with his outstanding collection of furniture, ceramics and paintings spanning three centuries. The house is also famous for the 1953 account of a plumber's apprentice who claimed to have seen a column of Roman soldiers marching through the cellar.
shortDescription: Historic York house beside the Minster with period interiors and a famous Roman ghost story.

name: Tredegar House
description: Tredegar House is one of the finest late 17th-century mansions in Wales, set within 90 acres of beautiful parkland on the outskirts of Newport. Home to the Morgan family for over 500 years, the house features lavish state rooms and restored service areas. The grounds include formal gardens, an orangery, and a lakeside walk.
shortDescription: One of Wales's finest 17th-century mansions with lavish interiors set within 90 acres of parkland.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 Children)

name: Trelissick Garden
description: Trelissick Garden is a beautiful and varied garden occupying a commanding position above the Fal estuary near Truro. Tender and exotic plants thrive in the mild Cornish climate, while the surrounding parkland and woodland walks offer sweeping views of the river. A gallery showcases the work of Cornish artists, and the King Harry Ferry operates nearby.
shortDescription: Subtropical garden above the Fal estuary with exotic planting and sweeping river views.

name: Trengwainton Garden
description: Trengwainton Garden is a lush subtropical paradise near Penzance, sheltered by ancient woodland and warmed by the Gulf Stream. The garden is renowned for its walled gardens featuring a series of small compartments originally built to grow exotic fruits and vegetables. Magnolias, camellias, tree ferns and rhododendrons thrive here, and many rare species were first grown in Britain at Trengwainton from seeds collected by plant hunters in the Himalayas and Burma.
shortDescription: Subtropical garden near Penzance with rare plants, walled gardens and sweeping views across Mount's Bay.

name: Trerice
description: Trerice is an intimate Elizabethan manor house hidden in a quiet valley near Newquay. Built in 1573 by Sir John Arundell, the house retains its original ornate plasterwork ceilings and a magnificent hall window of 576 panes. The surrounding garden includes an orchard of Cornish apple varieties and a sunny knot garden.
shortDescription: Hidden Elizabethan manor near Newquay with ornate plasterwork and a Cornish apple orchard.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Tudor Merchant's House
description: Tudor Merchant's House is a late 15th-century townhouse on Quay Hill in Tenby, built for a prosperous merchant when the town was a thriving trading port. The three-storey building retains its original chimney and traces of decorative wall paintings. It offers a vivid glimpse of domestic and commercial life in Tudor Wales.
shortDescription: A late 15th-century merchant's townhouse in Tenby with original features and Tudor wall paintings.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 adult and up to 3 children)

name: Tyntesfield
description: Tyntesfield is a spectacular Victorian Gothic Revival mansion built for the Gibbs family, who made their fortune from Peruvian guano. The house retains virtually all of its original contents, including Gothic furniture, stained glass, and a private chapel rivalling a small cathedral. The 540-acre estate features terraced gardens, a kitchen garden, and extensive parkland with far-reaching views across North Somerset.
shortDescription: A lavish Victorian Gothic mansion with original contents and a chapel of cathedral proportions.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Uffington White Horse
description: The Uffington White Horse is a highly stylised prehistoric hill figure, 110 metres long, cut into the chalk of the Berkshire Downs. Dating to the late Bronze Age around 3,000 years ago, it is the oldest chalk figure in Britain. The surrounding landscape includes the dramatic flat-topped Dragon Hill and the ancient earthwork of Uffington Castle.
shortDescription: Prehistoric chalk hill figure on the Berkshire Downs, the oldest of its kind in Britain at 3,000 years old.

name: Ullswater
description: Ullswater is the second largest lake in the Lake District, stretching seven and a half miles through three dramatic reaches. The National Trust cares for much of the surrounding land including the famous Glenridding Common, Glencoyne and the slopes of Helvellyn. The shores provide the setting for Wordsworth's daffodils and offer some of the finest scenery in England.
shortDescription: The Lake District's second largest lake with dramatic mountain scenery and Wordsworth's daffodil shores.

name: Uppark House and Garden
description: Uppark is an elegant 17th-century house set high on the South Downs with panoramic views to the Solent. Meticulously restored after a devastating fire in 1989, the house showcases Georgian interiors, a renowned doll's house collection, and fine paintings. H.G. Wells spent part of his childhood here when his mother was housekeeper.
shortDescription: An elegant South Downs house with Georgian interiors, restored after a dramatic 1989 fire.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Upper Wharfedale
description: Upper Wharfedale is a spectacular valley in the Yorkshire Dales, stretching from Buckden to the head of the dale. The area features dramatic limestone scenery, traditional hay meadows, ancient woodlands and the picturesque village of Buckden.
shortDescription: A spectacular limestone valley with traditional meadows in the Yorkshire Dales.

name: Upton House and Gardens
description: Upton House is a late-17th-century country house remodelled in the 1920s-30s by the 2nd Viscount Bearsted to display his world-class art collection. The house contains masterpieces by El Greco, Canaletto, Stubbs, and Hogarth, alongside Brussels tapestries and Chelsea porcelain. The terraced gardens cascade dramatically down a hidden valley to a mirror pool, with a 1930s bog garden and herbaceous borders.
shortDescription: A country house with a world-class art collection and dramatic terraced gardens in a hidden valley.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Waddesdon Manor
description: Waddesdon Manor is a French Renaissance-style chateau built by Baron Ferdinand de Rothschild in the 1870s. The house contains one of the finest collections of French decorative arts in the world, alongside Old Master paintings and Sèvres porcelain. The grounds include a parterre, aviary, and seasonal art exhibitions.
shortDescription: A Rothschild-built French Renaissance chateau with world-class decorative arts and beautiful gardens.

name: Wallington
description: Wallington is a grand yet welcoming country house set in beautiful grounds in rural Northumberland. The interior features Pre-Raphaelite paintings, fine porcelain and an impressive central hall decorated with murals depicting the history of the region. The walled garden, woodland walks and adventure playground make it a rewarding place for families.
shortDescription: A welcoming Northumberland country house with Pre-Raphaelite murals and beautiful walled gardens.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Wasdale
description: Wasdale is one of the most dramatic valleys in the Lake District, home to England's deepest lake (Wast Water) and highest mountain (Scafell Pike). The iconic view of the Wasdale Head screes plunging into the dark waters of the lake is one of the most photographed scenes in the national park. The valley is a key starting point for ascents of Scafell Pike and Great Gable.
shortDescription: England's deepest lake and highest mountain meet in this dramatic and iconic Lakeland valley.

name: Washington Old Hall
description: Washington Old Hall is a modest stone manor house that was home to the ancestors of George Washington, the first President of the United States. The present building dates mainly from the 17th century and contains period furnishings along with displays charting the Washington family's history. A small formal garden complements the house.
shortDescription: The ancestral home of George Washington's family, a 17th-century stone manor in Tyne and Wear.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)
Family (1 Adult and up to 3 children)

name: Watersmeet
description: Watersmeet is a beauty spot in the Exmoor valley where the East Lyn River and Hoar Oak Water converge in a deep, wooded gorge. The 19th-century fishing lodge at the confluence now serves as a National Trust tea room and information point. The surrounding ancient oak woodland, rich with mosses and ferns, is threaded with riverside walking trails.
shortDescription: Exmoor beauty spot where two rivers meet in a deep, ancient oak woodland gorge.

name: Wellbrook Beetling Mill
description: Wellbrook Beetling Mill is the last working linen beetling mill in Northern Ireland, set in a wooded glen near Cookstown in County Tyrone. The mill demonstrates the final stage of linen production where the cloth is pounded by water-driven hammers to produce its characteristic sheen. Guided tours explain the process and the importance of the linen industry to Ulster.
shortDescription: The last working linen beetling mill in Northern Ireland, demonstrating the traditional cloth-finishing process.

name: Wentworth Castle Gardens
description: Wentworth Castle Gardens is a 26-hectare designed landscape near Barnsley, created by the Strafford family from the early 18th century as a rival to neighbouring Wentworth Woodhouse. The gardens contain a nationally important collection of rhododendrons and camellias, a Victorian conservatory and the sham castle folly that gives the estate its name. The parkland was designed to impress, with grand vistas, ornamental buildings and one of the finest collections of garden monuments in England.
shortDescription: Grand 18th-century designed landscape near Barnsley with outstanding rhododendrons and a sham castle folly.

name: West Green House Garden
description: West Green House Garden is an elegant 18th-century walled garden surrounding a Georgian manor house in Hampshire. The garden combines formal design with imaginative modern planting, featuring a water garden, topiary, a neo-classical column and productive kitchen garden. The garden is best known for its summer opera season held in the grounds.
shortDescription: Elegant Georgian walled garden in Hampshire renowned for its summer opera season and formal planting.

name: West Wycombe Park
description: West Wycombe Park is a Palladian country house set within landscaped grounds created by Sir Francis Dashwood in the 18th century. The house contains a magnificent collection of tapestries, furniture and paintings, while the grounds feature a lake, temples and classical follies inspired by Dashwood's Grand Tour of Italy. The estate also includes West Wycombe village and the iconic golden ball atop St Lawrence's Church on the hill above.
shortDescription: Palladian mansion with 18th-century landscaped park, classical temples and the Dashwood family's Grand Tour collections.

name: Westbury Court Garden
description: Westbury Court Garden is a rare surviving example of a late 17th-century Dutch water garden, one of the finest of its kind in England. Originally laid out between 1696 and 1705, the garden features long formal canals flanked by clipped hedges, a tall pavilion and a restored summer house. After falling into neglect, the National Trust restored the garden using the original account books of its creator, Maynard Colchester. The walled garden contains varieties of fruit, vegetables and flowers known to have been grown in English gardens before 1700.
shortDescription: Rare late 17th-century Dutch water garden with formal canals, clipped hedges and a period walled garden.

name: Westwood Manor
description: Westwood Manor is a small stone manor house near Bradford-on-Avon with origins in the 15th century, modified and extended over the following 200 years to create a fascinating patchwork of medieval, Tudor and Stuart architecture. The interior contains remarkable late Gothic and Jacobean plasterwork, stained glass and fine examples of 17th-century panelling. The manor was saved from dereliction by Edgar Lister in 1911, who filled it with his collection of keyboard instruments and period furnishings, creating a home that feels authentically lived in across the centuries.
shortDescription: Stone manor near Bradford-on-Avon spanning five centuries of architecture, with fine plasterwork and stained glass.

name: Wheal Coates
description: Wheal Coates is a dramatic ruined tin mine perched on the clifftops near St Agnes, one of the most photographed industrial heritage sites in Cornwall. The engine houses and chimney stacks, dating from the 1870s, stand as striking monuments to Cornwall's mining past. The site is part of the Cornwall and West Devon Mining Landscape World Heritage Site.
shortDescription: Iconic clifftop tin mine ruins near St Agnes, part of Cornwall's Mining World Heritage Site.

name: White Cliffs of Dover
description: The White Cliffs of Dover are an iconic landmark of the English coastline, rising to 110 metres above the sea. The chalky grassland is home to rare wildflowers and butterflies, while the clifftop trails offer dramatic views across the English Channel to France. The Fan Bay Deep Shelter, a WWII tunnels network, can also be visited.
shortDescription: England's iconic chalk cliffs with clifftop walks, rare wildlife, and views across to France.

name: White Mill
description: White Mill is a charming 18th-century corn mill on the River Stour near Sturminster Marshall in Dorset. The mill retains its original wooden machinery and still grinds flour on open days. Set in a peaceful riverside meadow, it provides a window into the area's agricultural heritage.
shortDescription: Working 18th-century corn mill on the River Stour with original wooden machinery.

name: White Park Bay
description: White Park Bay is a stunning crescent of white sand stretching between two headlands on the north Antrim coast. The bay has Neolithic and Bronze Age archaeological significance, with flint tools and pottery found in its sand dunes. Chalk grassland above the beach supports rare plants and butterflies, and the bay is overlooked by dramatic limestone cliffs.
shortDescription: A sweeping crescent of white sand beach on the north Antrim coast with Neolithic archaeological remains.

name: Whitehaven Coast
description: The Whitehaven Coast stretches along the western edge of Cumbria, offering dramatic clifftop walks with views across the Irish Sea to the Isle of Man and Scotland. The Trust cares for stretches of this coastline including the restored Haig Colliery mining museum site at Whitehaven. Red sandstone cliffs, shingle beaches and industrial heritage combine along this underrated coastline.
shortDescription: Dramatic red sandstone cliffs on Cumbria's west coast with views to the Isle of Man.

name: Wicken Fen
description: Wicken Fen is one of Britain's oldest nature reserves and one of Europe's most important wetlands. This ancient fenland, the National Trust's first nature reserve acquired in 1899, supports over 9,000 species including rare butterflies, birds, and plants. The reserve offers boardwalk trails, a windpump, and boat trips, and is part of the Wicken Fen Vision to create a 53-square-kilometre nature reserve.
shortDescription: One of Britain's oldest nature reserves, a precious fenland wetland supporting over 9,000 species.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wightwick Manor
description: Wightwick Manor is a Victorian manor house built in the Arts and Crafts style, filled with an outstanding collection of Pre-Raphaelite art including works by Rossetti, Burne-Jones and William Morris. The house is complemented by beautiful gardens with topiary, terraces and a woodland garden. It offers one of the finest collections of Pre-Raphaelite paintings outside a public gallery.
shortDescription: A Victorian Arts and Crafts manor filled with Pre-Raphaelite masterpieces near Wolverhampton.

name: Wilderhope Manor
description: Wilderhope Manor is a fine Elizabethan manor house perched on the edge of Wenlock Edge in the Shropshire Hills, built in 1585 by Francis Smallman. The house retains its original plaster ceilings with elaborate decorative motifs and a handsome oak spiral staircase. During the Civil War, Major Thomas Smallman famously escaped Parliamentarian captors by leaping his horse from the limestone cliff behind the house — a spot still known as the Major's Leap. Now a YHA youth hostel, the manor is open for guided tours on certain days.
shortDescription: Elizabethan manor on Wenlock Edge with original plaster ceilings and a famous Civil War escape story.

name: Wimpole Estate
description: Wimpole Estate is Cambridgeshire's most impressive country house, an 18th-century mansion set in 3,000 acres of parkland, farmland, and gardens designed by Capability Brown. The house features grand interiors including a spectacular library and Baroque chapel. The estate includes a working Home Farm with rare breed animals, particularly popular with families.
shortDescription: Cambridgeshire's grandest country house with a Capability Brown park and a working rare breeds farm.

name: Winchester City Mill
description: Winchester City Mill is a working watermill straddling the River Itchen in the heart of Winchester. There has been a mill on this site since at least the Domesday Book of 1086. Restored to full working order, visitors can see flour being milled by waterpower and learn about 1,000 years of milling history.
shortDescription: A restored working watermill in the heart of Winchester with over 1,000 years of milling history.

name: Winkworth Arboretum
description: Winkworth Arboretum is a hillside woodland planted with over 1,000 different shrubs and trees from around the world. Created by Dr Wilfrid Fox in the early 20th century, the arboretum is renowned for its spectacular bluebell carpet in spring and vivid autumn foliage reflected in the lake. It covers 112 acres of the Surrey Hills.
shortDescription: A hillside arboretum with over 1,000 tree species, famous for spring bluebells and autumn colour.

name: Woodchester Park
description: Woodchester Park is a hidden valley estate in the Cotswolds containing a chain of five lakes surrounded by beech and oak woodland. The secluded valley conceals the unfinished Victorian Gothic mansion of Woodchester Mansion, abandoned mid-construction in the 1870s. The park is home to one of Britain's largest greater horseshoe bat colonies and offers peaceful walks through its wooded landscape.
shortDescription: Hidden Cotswold valley with five lakes, ancient woodland and an unfinished Victorian Gothic mansion.

name: Woolbeding Gardens
description: Woolbeding Gardens are a series of enchanting garden rooms surrounding an Elizabethan manor house in the South Downs. The gardens blend formal design with wild planting to dramatic effect, featuring a modern parterre, a riverside walk along the River Rother, and the spectacular Tulip Folly designed by Julian and Isabel Bannerman. Pre-booking is required as the gardens open only on selected days, and visitors arrive via a shuttle bus to preserve the tranquil atmosphere.
shortDescription: Enchanting garden rooms in the South Downs with the spectacular Tulip Folly and riverside walks.

name: Woolsthorpe Manor
description: Woolsthorpe Manor is the birthplace of Sir Isaac Newton, where he was born on Christmas Day 1642 and later developed his theories of light, gravity, and calculus. The modest 17th-century farmhouse still contains the room where Newton conducted his famous prism experiments. In the garden stands a descendant of the apple tree said to have inspired his theory of gravity.
shortDescription: The birthplace of Sir Isaac Newton, where he developed his groundbreaking scientific theories.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wordsworth House
description: Wordsworth House is the Georgian townhouse in Cockermouth where the poet William Wordsworth was born in 1770 and spent his formative childhood years. The house has been furnished to reflect life in the 1770s, with hands-on displays and costumed guides bringing the period to life. The riverside garden, which Wordsworth described in The Prelude, runs down to the River Derwent.
shortDescription: The Georgian birthplace of William Wordsworth in Cockermouth, furnished as it was in the 1770s.

pricing.label: Family (2 Adults and up to 3 children)

name: Wray Castle
description: Wray Castle is a Gothic Revival castle on the western shore of Windermere, built in the 1840s for a retired surgeon. The young Beatrix Potter spent a holiday here in 1882, sparking her lifelong love of the Lake District. The castle interior is largely unfurnished but hosts interactive displays and dressing-up activities for families.
shortDescription: A Gothic Revival castle on Windermere's shore where Beatrix Potter first fell in love with the Lakes.
